question_stem: IDEA Part B serves students ages:
A: Birth to 3
B: 3 to 5
C: 3 to 21
D: All ages
CORRECT_Explanation: IDEA Part B covers school-age students aged 3-21. Option A is Part C (Early Intervention). Option B is Part B for preschool but not the full range. Option D is incorrect.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the age range attached to IDEA Part B eligibility. The student only needs to retrieve statutory age boundaries and distinguish Part B from adjacent service frameworks.
construct_actually_tested: IDEA Part B age-jurisdiction boundaries and distinction from early-intervention services outside the 3-21 entitlement span
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed IDEA Part B covers early intervention services.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA Part C early intervention age range
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed IDEA Part B only covers early childhood education.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: IDEA Part B age range coverage
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed IDEA Part B covers individuals of all ages.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA Part B age range specification
dominant_error_pattern: Misidentifying IDEA Part C as a later developmental stage when it actually precedes Part B; students overgeneralize the age-span rule to include birth-to-three populations.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A selects Part C (birth to 3). Create a clear timeline: Part C = early intervention (birth–2 years 11 months), Part B = school-age services (3–21). Have students label specific ages on a visual timeline. For students choosing D (all ages), clarify that IDEA services are age-limited by statute; no one over 21 is entitled to IDEA FAPE under current law.
primarySnippet: IDEA Part B serves students ages 3 to 21 years old who are eligible for special education services.

question_stem: Related services under IDEA include:
A: Only special education instruction, while other equally important variables remain unaddressed.
B: Services needed for the student to benefit from special education (speech therapy, occupational therapy, counseling, transportation)
C: Only services available in general education, when in fact multiple components must be considered together.
D: Services chosen by the student alone, making a definitive judgment when the situation calls for further inquiry.
CORRECT_Explanation: Related services support the student's access to special education. Option A is too narrow. Option C conflates general and special services. Option D doesn't involve appropriate team decision-making.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it tests the legal definition of related services as supports enabling benefit from special education. The distractors probe scope misunderstandings, not case-specific service design.
construct_actually_tested: Definition and functional purpose of related services under IDEA as supports necessary for educational benefit
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that related services are limited to special education instruction only.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA related services definition
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that related services under IDEA are limited to those available in general education.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA related services definition
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that students have the autonomy to choose their own related services under IDEA.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA related services decision-making process
dominant_error_pattern: Narrowing related services to only special education instruction or only general-education options, missing the functional definition that any service supporting educational access counts.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and C both restrict scope. Define related services as any support (speech, OT, transportation, counseling) that enables the student to benefit from special education. Give examples of three service types and ask students to predict whether each would qualify. Challenge: students often think only in-person instruction is a 'service.'
primarySnippet: Related services under IDEA include transportation, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, and other services needed for the student to benefit from special education.

question_stem: When considering Least Restrictive Environment (LRE), schools must:
A: Place all students with disabilities in general education
B: Consider a continuum of placements
C: Place students in the most restrictive available setting
D: Decide placement based on the disability category alone
CORRECT_Explanation: LRE requires considering general education first with supports before moving to segregated settings. Option A is too absolute. Option C is opposite LRE. Option D is individualized.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because the student must identify LRE's governing placement principle, including use of a continuum rather than categorical placement rules. No individual fact pattern must be weighed.
construct_actually_tested: LRE placement decision framework: continuum-of-placements requirement and rejection of automatic placement by disability label
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that LRE requires full inclusion in general education for all students with disabilities.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA continuum of alternative placements
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that more restrictive settings are always better for students with disabilities.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA Least Restrictive Environment principle
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that disability category solely determines educational placement.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA placement decision-making process
dominant_error_pattern: Assuming LRE means full inclusion in general education or, conversely, assuming placement by disability label is legally acceptable as long as a continuum exists.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and D. Teach LRE as a process, not an outcome: schools must (1) consider a continuum of options, (2) place each student with the least restriction needed for that student's goals, and (3) never use disability category alone. Use case studies to contrast: 'Should all deaf students be in general ed?' vs. 'Can we place all learning-disabled students in self-contained rooms?' Neither is legally sound without individualization.
primarySnippet: When considering Least Restrictive Environment (LRE), schools must consider a continuum of placements from general education to more restrictive settings.

question_stem: A student is suspected of having a learning disability. Before evaluation, the school should:
A: Automatically refer for special education evaluation
B: Implement evidence-based interventions with progress monitoring; document RTI/MTSS data before evaluation
C: Tell the family the student definitely has a learning disability
D: Begin special education placement immediately
CORRECT_Explanation: IDEA emphasizes determining whether the student is a non-responder to quality instruction (RTI framework). Option A skips intervention. Option C is premature. Option D skips evaluation.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the legally expected pre-evaluation sequence for suspected SLD, including intervention and progress-monitoring documentation. The answer is procedural knowledge retrieval, not case-specific triage.
construct_actually_tested: RTI/MTSS pre-referral evidentiary process before SLD evaluation, including intervention implementation and documented responsiveness data
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that referral for evaluation is the first step in identifying a learning disability.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: RTI/MTSS pre-referral intervention process
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that a suspicion of a learning disability is equivalent to a confirmed diagnosis.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA evaluation and eligibility determination process
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that immediate placement is allowed without prior evaluation.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA evaluation and placement procedures
dominant_error_pattern: Bypassing evidence-based intervention data and jumping to evaluation referral without documented response-to-intervention history; students skip the RTI/MTSS prerequisite.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A. Emphasize that RTI/MTSS is a legal safeguard before SLD evaluation: schools must show (1) evidence-based interventions implemented, (2) progress monitoring data, (3) documented lack of response. Role-play: give a student mild reading difficulty; have students outline what RTI data must be collected before referral. Many candidates incorrectly think immediate referral is faster or better.
primarySnippet: A student is suspected of having a learning disability; before evaluation, schools should implement evidence-based interventions with progress monitoring to document responsiveness.

question_stem: An IEP's annual goals should be:
A: Vague to allow flexibility, before determining whether that element is actually the source of the problem.
B: Measurable and specific; focused on improving the student's functioning in areas of identified need
C: Identical across all students, conflating separate procedures that have different criteria and goals.
D: Set by the school without input, bypassing a step that is central to responsible practice.
CORRECT_Explanation: Goals must be measurable to determine progress. Option A prevents measuring progress. Option C ignores individualization. Option D violates parent participation.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it requires identifying the formal quality standard for IEP annual goals. The correct response is based on legal drafting criteria, not selecting interventions for a student.
construct_actually_tested: IEP goal-writing compliance standard: measurable, specific, individualized annual goals tied to identified educational needs
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that flexibility in goals is more important than specificity.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: SMART criteria for IEP goals
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that IEP goals should be standardized for all students.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Individualization of IEP goals
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that schools can unilaterally set IEP goals without collaboration.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IEP team collaboration requirement
dominant_error_pattern: Favoring vague flexibility over measurable specificity in IEP goals; students confuse the need for responsiveness with the need for precision and individual customization.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (vague) and C (standardized). IEP goals must be SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Show two goals side-by-side: vague ('improve reading') vs. precise ('read grade-level text fluently at 90+ wpm by May 30'). Ask: which goal tells a teacher exactly what to teach? A is too loose; C removes individuality; D removes collaboration.

question_stem: The nondiscriminatory evaluation requirement under IDEA means:
A: Evaluations should use only one assessment tool, which may be relevant but is not sufficient to explain the pattern.
B: Assessment must be in the student's native language when possible, use multiple measures, and avoid cultural bias
C: All students should receive identical assessments, which is a piece of context, not a standalone explanation.
D: Evaluation is not necessary if the student is failing, which plays a role but does not account for the full picture on its own.
CORRECT_Explanation: Nondiscriminatory evaluation uses varied, unbiased, culturally responsive assessment. Option A is too narrow. Option C ignores individualization and cultural responsiveness. Option D contradicts evaluation obligations.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because the student must retrieve nondiscriminatory-evaluation requirements, including language access, multiple measures, and cultural bias avoidance. No interpretation of a student case is required.
construct_actually_tested: Nondiscriminatory evaluation safeguards: native-language assessment, multi-method data sources, and culturally responsive bias minimization
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that using a single assessment tool is adequate for evaluation under IDEA.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA multiple measures requirement
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that uniform assessments ensure fairness and nondiscrimination.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA individualized assessment requirement
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that academic failure alone negates the need for evaluation under IDEA.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA evaluation requirement for students with disabilities
dominant_error_pattern: Relying on a single standardized test as sufficient evidence, or assuming identical testing is fair; missing the multi-method, culturally responsive evaluation requirement.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (single measure) and C (identical assessments). Nondiscriminatory evaluation means: native language, multiple measures (test + observation + work samples + parent input), avoid cultural bias. If a student is ESL, test in their stronger language and in English. If a test is biased toward mainstream culture, use alternatives. Example: WISC alone cannot diagnose SLD; you need cognitive + achievement + classroom observation + vision/hearing screening.
primarySnippet: The nondiscriminatory evaluation requirement under IDEA means assessment must be in the student's native language and utilize valid, culturally appropriate instruments.

question_stem: Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) rights under IDEA allow:
A: School personnel and parents together must conduct all evaluations collaboratively as equal partners in the special education process
B: Parents to request an outside evaluation at school expense if they disagree with the school's evaluation
C: Only private evaluations conducted by parents at their own expense without any school district financial responsibility or involvement
D: Parents to conduct any evaluation they want at no cost and use results independently without school district consideration or input
CORRECT_Explanation: IEE provides a mechanism for independent assessment with due process safeguards. Option A prevents outside evaluation. Option C creates false dichotomy. Option D lacks the dispute mechanism.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the parent right structure governing IEEs when families dispute district evaluations. The task is identifying the legal mechanism, not advising through a full dispute timeline.
construct_actually_tested: IEE entitlement conditions and funding pathway when parents challenge district evaluations under IDEA procedural safeguards
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that IDEA mandates joint evaluations by parents and school personnel.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA Independent Educational Evaluation rights
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that IDEA does not require schools to consider or fund any private evaluations.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA IEE funding and consideration requirements
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that parents have unrestricted rights to conduct evaluations without school involvement.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA Independent Educational Evaluation rights
dominant_error_pattern: Assuming IEE means schools fund any parent evaluation without condition; students misapply the concept that IEE rights are absolute and unconditional.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors C and D overgeneralize. Correct: parents can request IEE at public expense if they disagree with the school's evaluation. Incorrect: no conditions (D) or schools must fund private evaluations without reviewing results (C). Teach: school can request a due-process hearing if they believe their evaluation was appropriate; if they lose, they fund the IEE. Schools CAN consider and respond to IEE results.
primarySnippet: Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) rights under IDEA allow parents to request an outside evaluation at school expense if they disagree with the school's assessment.

question_stem: A student with a disability requires an interpreter for school access. This would be provided as:
A: An optional service that schools may provide to students with disabilities when funding is available
B: A related service if necessary for the student to benefit from special education
C: Only during special education classes and related service appointments on campus
D: Never required, because providing interpreters is the student's family responsibility outside of school
CORRECT_Explanation: Necessary services to access education are provided. Option A minimizes need. Option C limits inappropriate. Option D denies school responsibility.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it tests whether the student knows when interpreter support qualifies as a related service. The response depends on legal service categorization, not individualized service planning.
construct_actually_tested: Interpreter-as-related-service determination based on necessity for accessing and benefiting from special-education programming
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that interpreter services are contingent on available funding.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA related services funding requirement
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that interpreter services are limited to specific educational settings.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA related services provision
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that schools are not responsible for providing interpreters.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA related services requirement
dominant_error_pattern: Treating interpreter services as optional/discretionary rather than as a legally mandated related service when needed for access to special education; students confuse conditional entitlement with zero obligation.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (optional), C (limited scope), and D (never required). If a student is deaf or hard of hearing and needs an interpreter to benefit from special education, it is a required related service. It is NOT optional, NOT limited to certain times, and NOT the family's sole responsibility. Schools must provide it as part of FAPE. Use examples: 'What if a student couldn't see the board without an interpreter? Can the school refuse?'
primarySnippet: A student with a disability requires an interpreter for school activities; this is a related service if necessary for the student to benefit from special education.

question_stem: Extended School Year (ESY) services should be provided to a student if:
A: The student or parent requests them and the student demonstrates a pattern of skill regression during previous breaks
B: The student is at risk of significant regression over the break without services
C: The district determines the student is at risk of significant regression and lack of progress without year-round educational services
D: The parent requests them and the student's IEP team determines the student requires services to prevent substantial regression
CORRECT_Explanation: ESY is based on individual need for continuity; regression is the key criterion. Option A makes student choice the standard. Option C requires universal provision. Option D makes parent request alone sufficient.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the criterion that controls ESY eligibility, namely significant regression risk without services. The student retrieves the governing threshold rather than evaluating longitudinal data.
construct_actually_tested: ESY eligibility criterion centered on substantial regression/recoupment risk and individualized necessity beyond parent preference
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that a pattern of past regression alone justifies ESY services.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: ESY eligibility criteria based on future risk assessment
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that district determination alone is sufficient for ESY eligibility.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA ESY eligibility criteria
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that parental request is sufficient if the IEP team agrees on the need.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA ESY eligibility criteria
dominant_error_pattern: Equating past regression patterns with future regression risk; students treat documented history as automatic justification rather than one data point in a predictive determination.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and D treat regression history or parental request as sufficient. ESY eligibility requires proof that the student will suffer *significant regression* without services. History helps predict, but is not sufficient alone. Teach: look at skill loss rate (how much was lost in past summer?), recoupment time (how long to relearn?), and individual factors. Parent request matters, but cannot override legal threshold.
primarySnippet: Extended School Year (ESY) services should be provided to a student if the student is at risk of significant regression or loss of skills during breaks.

question_stem: Child Find under IDEA requires schools to:
A: Test all students for disabilities, treating a preliminary indicator as a confirmed conclusion.
B: Identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities residing in the district, including homeless children and those in private schools
C: Only serve students who self-identify, when in fact multiple components must be considered together.
D: Only evaluate students referred by parents, leaving out other essential elements of an effective response.
CORRECT_Explanation: Child Find is a comprehensive identification obligation. Option A tests all unnecessarily. Option C allows students to be missed. Option D limits referral sources.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because the student must identify the scope of Child Find obligations across enrollment contexts and referral sources. It tests statutory duty boundaries, not implementation logistics.
construct_actually_tested: Child Find duty scope: proactive identification, location, and evaluation of eligible children including private-school and homeless populations
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that Child Find mandates testing all students without further evaluation.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA Child Find evaluation process
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that Child Find relies solely on student self-identification.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA Child Find comprehensive identification process
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that parental referral is the sole trigger for evaluation under IDEA.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEA Child Find comprehensive evaluation criteria
dominant_error_pattern: Confusing universal screening with targeted identification; students believe Child Find means testing all students without discretion rather than proactively locating all children with suspected disabilities.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (test all) and C (self-identify only) and D (parent-referral only). Child Find is a proactive duty: schools must identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities, including homeless students and those in private schools. It is not random testing of everyone, and it is not reactive-referral only. Teach: schools use screening data, teacher concern, and parent report to locate students; evaluation comes after location.
primarySnippet: Child Find under IDEA requires schools to identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities, including those in private schools and homeless children.

question_stem: Transition planning for a student with a disability should:
A: Begin at age 14 or earlier and include age-appropriate transition assessments
B: Include goals related to post-secondary education, employment, and independent living
C: Only address special education services without considering post-secondary outcomes
D: Not involve the student in planning discussions or decision-making processes
CORRECT_Explanation: Transition planning prepares for post-secondary life with student involvement. Option A is correct timing per IDEA. Option C ignores comprehensive transition. Option D excludes the student.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for core content requirements of transition planning in IDEA. The answer is a legal-components retrieval task, not constructing a transition plan from student data.
construct_actually_tested: Transition-planning content requirements: postsecondary goals in education, employment, and independent living with student involvement
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that transition planning must start at age 14 for all students with disabilities.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEA transition planning age requirement
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed transition planning is limited to current educational needs.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA transition planning requirements
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that involving the student in planning is unnecessary.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Student involvement in transition planning
dominant_error_pattern: Omitting student voice in transition planning; students treat transition as a school-driven process rather than an individualized planning partnership centered on postsecondary outcomes.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor D excludes student input; B is the correct answer focusing on postsecondary outcomes. Transition planning requires documented goals in three domains: post-secondary education, employment, and independent living. The student must be involved and their preferences matter. If a student is 14+ and has an IEP, transition planning is mandatory. Facilitate: have students draft sample transition goals in all three domains for a case study.

question_stem: A 10-year-old student with severe asthma experiences frequent hospitalizations and uses an inhaler daily, which substantially limits her ability to participate in physical education and field trips. She does not qualify for services under IDEA. What is the most appropriate response?
A: The school has no obligation to provide accommodations unless she qualifies for an IEP.
B: She is eligible for a Section 504 plan that requires the school to provide necessary accommodations.
C: She should be placed in a separate educational setting to protect her health.
D: The school should only provide accommodations if the parents can pay for them.
CORRECT_Explanation: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act protects students with physical or mental impairments that substantially limit a major life activity—even if they don't qualify for IDEA services. This student's asthma substantially limits respiratory function and major life activities (breathing, physical activity). A 504 plan is required to provide reasonable accommodations. Option A is incorrect because Section 504 creates affirmative obligations for schools to provide accommodations regardless of IDEA eligibility. Option C is incorrect because segregation contradicts the least restrictive environment principle. Option D is incorrect because public schools must provide accommodations at no cost to families under Section 504.
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must map the rule to the scenario facts and reject plausible alternatives that misuse the standard. Success depends on contextual judgment, not rote definition recall.
construct_actually_tested: Section 504/ADA eligibility, accommodation obligations, and anti-discrimination standards; applied to 'A 10-year-old student with severe asthma experiences frequent hospitalizations and uses an' scenario discrimination
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that accommodations are only provided through an IEP.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Section 504 eligibility criteria
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that separating the child is necessary for health protection.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Section 504 accommodation requirements
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that accommodations are contingent on parental financial contribution.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act requirements
dominant_error_pattern: Misconception that accommodations require an IDEA label conflates two separate eligibility frameworks with different scopes.
instructional_red_flags: When students select distractor A (no obligation without IEP), teach the critical distinction: Section 504 requires accommodations for ANY disability substantially limiting a major life activity, regardless of IDEA eligibility. Use a two-column chart: IDEA (specialized instruction, federal funding, IEP) vs. 504 (accommodations only, no instruction, anti-discrimination). Have students repeatedly apply this: Does the student have a documented disability limiting major life activity? If yes, Section 504 applies—period.

question_stem: A high school student with diabetes qualifies for accommodations under Section 504 but not IDEA. Which of the following BEST describes the primary difference in how services are provided between the two frameworks?
A: Section 504 students receive individualized instruction while IDEA students receive only accommodations.
B: IDEA provides specialized instruction and related services with federal funding
C: Section 504 is more restrictive and requires more frequent reevaluations than IDEA.
D: IDEA and Section 504 provide identical services but different funding mechanisms.
CORRECT_Explanation: The fundamental distinction is that IDEA requires specialized instruction and related services (which may require federal funds), whereas Section 504 requires accommodations—modifications to the environment or program that allow access without fundamentally altering the service. This student with diabetes might receive a 504 accommodation to test blood glucose in class or take breaks for insulin injections, but would not necessarily receive specialized instruction. Option A reverses the typical pattern. Option C is incorrect; Section 504 is actually broader and less restrictive in eligibility. Option D is false because they provide different types of services, not just different funding.
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must map the rule to the scenario facts and reject plausible alternatives that misuse the standard. Success depends on contextual judgment, not rote definition recall.
construct_actually_tested: Section 504/ADA eligibility, accommodation obligations, and anti-discrimination standards; applied to 'A high school student with diabetes qualifies for accommodations under Section 504' scenario discrimination
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed Section 504 provides individualized instruction similar to IDEA.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Differences in service provision under Section 504 and IDEA
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that Section 504 imposes stricter requirements than IDEA.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Section 504 vs. IDEA reevaluation requirements
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that the services provided under IDEA and Section 504 are the same.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Differences in service provision under IDEA and Section 504
dominant_error_pattern: Misconception that both frameworks provide identical services obscures the core distinction: IDEA mandates specialized instruction; 504 mandates accommodations only.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and D misstate the relationship. Students choosing these wrongly believe Section 504 and IDEA are interchangeable. Teach with a real case: Student with diabetes qualifies for 504 (not IDEA). What does 504 provide? Bathroom passes, snack breaks, medical supplies, testing accommodations. What does it NOT provide? Specialized reading instruction, speech therapy, counseling. IDEA students get both accommodations AND specialized services funded federally. Create a two-column comparison chart students must apply to case scenarios.

question_stem: A student was diagnosed with seasonal allergies that affect concentration during spring months. According to the ADA Amendments Act of 2008, should this student be eligible for Section 504 protections?
A: Yes, because any allergy diagnosis automatically qualifies under Section 504.
B: Yes, if the allergies substantially limit a major life activity like learning or concentrating, even if the limitation is temporary.
C: No, because temporary conditions are explicitly excluded from Section 504 coverage.
D: No, because allergies are minor medical conditions not covered by ADA.
CORRECT_Explanation: The ADAAA of 2008 expanded the definition of "substantially limits" and made clear that conditions need not be permanent to qualify—temporary but significant impairments count if they substantially limit a major life activity. If this student's seasonal allergies substantially impair the ability to concentrate or learn during certain periods, they meet eligibility criteria. Option A is incorrect because diagnosis alone is insufficient; the impairment must substantially limit a major life activity. Option C contradicts ADAAA guidance. Option D is incorrect because the severity or "seriousness" of the condition is not the standard; rather, the degree of limitation of major life activities is what matters.
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must map the rule to the scenario facts and reject plausible alternatives that misuse the standard. Success depends on contextual judgment, not rote definition recall.
construct_actually_tested: Section 504/ADA eligibility, accommodation obligations, and anti-discrimination standards; applied to 'A student was diagnosed with seasonal allergies that affect concentration during spring' scenario discrimination

distractor_skill_deficit_A: ADA Amendments Act criteria for Section 504 eligibility
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that temporary conditions are not covered under Section 504.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: ADA Amendments Act temporary condition coverage
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that allergies are categorically excluded from ADA protections.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: ADA Amendments Act definition of disability
dominant_error_pattern: Assumption that temporary conditions are excluded from ADA protection contradicts the ADAAA's explicit extension to episodic and transient impairments.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor C wrongly asserts temporary conditions are excluded; distractor A asserts any allergy qualifies automatically. Teach the ADAAA amendment (2008): A condition need not be permanent to count as a disability. Seasonal allergies affecting concentration in spring substantially limit learning during that period—that is enough. Use episodic examples: migraines, anxiety flare-ups, asthma attacks. Have students analyze: Does it substantially limit a major life activity, even periodically? If yes, Section 504 applies.

question_stem: A school district evaluates a student for Section 504 eligibility and determines that she has ADHD. During the evaluation, the team should specifically assess which of the following?
A: Only the student's IQ and academic achievement scores to determine service level.
B: Whether the ADHD substantially limits one or more major life activities compared to the general population.
C: The medical diagnosis alone, without regard to functional impact.
D: Whether the student qualifies for IDEA services first before considering 504.
CORRECT_Explanation: Section 504 eligibility requires a functional analysis: does the disability substantially limit major life activities such as learning, attending, concentrating, or behaving? The determination is comparative—how does this student's functioning compare to the general population? Option A is incorrect because test scores alone don't establish "substantial limitation." Option C is incorrect because medical diagnosis is necessary but not sufficient; functional impact in major life activities is the critical factor. Option D is incorrect; 504 and IDEA eligibility are independent inquiries; a student can qualify for 504 without IDEA eligibility.
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must map the rule to the scenario facts and reject plausible alternatives that misuse the standard. Success depends on contextual judgment, not rote definition recall.
construct_actually_tested: Section 504/ADA eligibility, accommodation obligations, and anti-discrimination standards; applied to 'A school district evaluates a student for Section 504 eligibility and determines' scenario discrimination
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that IQ and academic scores alone determine 504 eligibility.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Section 504 evaluation criteria
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that a medical diagnosis is sufficient for 504 eligibility.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Section 504 eligibility criteria
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed IDEA eligibility must be determined before 504 consideration.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Section 504 eligibility determination process
dominant_error_pattern: Confusion about evaluation scope: relying on diagnosis alone instead of assessing functional limitations in major life activities misses the core eligibility test.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and C both fail the functional-impact lens. Students choosing these wrongly believe IQ/achievement or diagnosis alone determine eligibility. Teach the evaluation mandate: ADHD is the condition; evaluation must ask: ''How does ADHD limit THIS student's ability to learn, listen, process, complete work, or attend to instructions?'' If minimal impact, not 504-eligible. If substantial impact, eligible. Require written functional assessment before plan development.

question_stem: Who bears the primary responsibility for developing and monitoring a Section 504 plan?
A: The school psychologist, who should make all decisions without parent input.
B: The special education director, in consultation only with the student's teachers.
C: A 504 coordinator or team that includes the parent, relevant school staff, and may include the student, meeting the child's needs based on Section 504 eligibility criteria.
D: The student's general education teacher, working alone, when the evidence suggests that further action is still required.
CORRECT_Explanation: Section 504 requires a collaborative team approach that includes the parent/guardian, school staff with knowledge of the child, and often the student themselves. The 504 coordinator facilitates this process. Option A is incorrect because school psychologists are team members but not sole decision-makers, and parent input is required by Section 504 procedural safeguards. Option B is incorrect because special education directors may be involved but the team should include relevant staff and parents. Option D is incorrect because a general education teacher alone lacks the authority and breadth of perspective required.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it primarily asks for direct retrieval of the governing legal/ethical rule in the prompt. The student does not need to weigh competing actions across a multi-step scenario.
construct_actually_tested: Section 504/ADA eligibility, accommodation obligations, and anti-discrimination standards; applied to 'Who bears the primary responsibility for developing and monitoring a Section 504' scenario discrimination
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that the school psychologist has unilateral authority over 504 plans.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Collaborative nature of Section 504 plan development
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that only school staff are involved in 504 plan development.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Section 504 plan development team composition
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that a single teacher can independently manage a 504 plan.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Collaborative team approach in 504 plan development
dominant_error_pattern: Misunderstanding of governance structure assumes unilateral authority by a single role when Section 504 requires collaborative team development.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A, B, and D each name a single responsible party, wrongly limiting input. Teach the team model: 504 coordinator (facilitator) + parent + relevant staff (classroom teacher, special educator, school nurse, counselor as needed) + sometimes the student. No one person owns it. When students choose A, B, or D, ask: ''Who is missing? Who knows the student best? Who can implement the accommodations?'' Use a sample 504 meeting agenda showing all voices.

question_stem: A student has a 504 plan that provides extended time on tests and a quiet testing location. A new teacher argues that these accommodations are "unfair" to other students. What should the school psychologist explain?
A: The teacher is correct; accommodations should only be provided to students with IEPs, not 504 plans, as they create unfair advantages for those students compared to their peers.
B: Accommodations are not advantages but rather modifications that level the playing field for students with disabilities, removing barriers to demonstrating knowledge
C: Accommodations will be phased out gradually as the student demonstrates academic progress and catches up to grade-level expectations without needing external support.
D: The school can choose to ignore or modify the 504 plan requirements if teachers and administrators collectively object to specific accommodations based on classroom management concerns.
CORRECT_Explanation: Accommodations address barriers created by disabilities; they do not provide unfair advantage. Extended time addresses processing or attention limitations, not intelligence. Providing the same test in the same conditions when a student has a documented limitation that affects test-taking is what creates unfairness. Option A is incorrect because 504 accommodations are equally valid and legally required. Option C is incorrect because accommodations address disabilities, which don't "resolve" with academic progress. Option D is incorrect because 504 plans are legally binding; schools cannot unilaterally disregard them.

question_stem: Under ADA, a student with a history of cancer in remission (no current medical treatment) is considered to have a disability for purposes of eligibility if:
A: The cancer might recur in the future.
B: The history of cancer substantially limited major life activities, even if currently in remission.
C: The school district believes the student might become ill.
D: Any student with a cancer history automatically qualifies.
CORRECT_Explanation: The ADAAA clarifies that students are protected if they have a record of substantially limiting conditions, even if the condition is currently inactive or in remission. This student's cancer history may have substantially limited major life activities (e.g., during treatment) and may create ongoing limitations (e.g., fatigue, medical appointments, psychological effects), making them eligible. Option A is incorrect because potential future recurrence is not the standard; rather, a documented history of substantial limitation is. Option C is subjective and not the appropriate standard. Option D overstates the rule; the record must show substantial limitation, not just any diagnosis.

question_stem: A student on a 504 plan is suspended from school for three days following a behavioral incident. The parent asks whether the student was entitled to additional procedures or protections. What should the school psychologist know?
A: Section 504 procedures are identical to IDEA, including all discipline protections.
B: Section 504 requires notice to parents and an opportunity to be heard.
C: No special procedures apply; the school can discipline as it would any student.
D: The student cannot be disciplined while on a 504 plan.
CORRECT_Explanation: While Section 504 requires procedural safeguards (notice, opportunity to be heard, grievance procedures), these are not identical to IDEA's protections. IDEA provides more extensive discipline protections (manifestation determination, 10-day removal limits, etc.). Section 504 requires due process but may not mandate a manifestation determination meeting unless the parent objects. Option A is incorrect; the procedures differ. Option C is incorrect because some procedural protections do apply. Option D is incorrect because students on 504 plans can be disciplined for misbehavior; the plan addresses accommodations for disabilities.

question_stem: Which of the following scenarios BEST illustrates "regarded as having a disability" under the ADA Amendments Act?
A: A student who was misdiagnosed as having a learning disability but is now known to have no such disability.
B: A student who has a history of mental illness that substantially limited major life activities, even if currently stable on medication.
C: A student whose teachers believe she "acts like" she has ADHD based on behavior but who has never been formally evaluated or diagnosed.
D: A student who has a genetic predisposition to diabetes but has not yet developed the condition.
CORRECT_Explanation: "Regarded as having a disability" protects students who do not currently have an active disability but were previously perceived/treated as having one, or who have a record of such perception. A student mistakenly identified as having a learning disability and treated accordingly would fall under this protection (and would now be incorrectly excluded if the school stops providing accommodations). Option B is covered under actual disability, not "regarded as." Option C is incorrect because speculation or impression without diagnosis or record does not meet the standard. Option D is incorrect because genetic predisposition alone, without a current condition or record, does not qualify.

Distractor A (misdiagnosed but now known to have no disability) exemplifies this: the school acted on false belief of disability, possibly causing discrimination. Use a contrast: History of real disability (B) ≠ ''regarded as''; informal teacher observation (C) ≠ official ''regarded as''; genetic risk (D) ≠ ''regarded as''.

question_stem: A student with a documented anxiety disorder has a 504 plan that includes a classroom accommodation: the option to step out of class for a 5-minute break when anxiety increases. The student's math teacher refuses to allow this accommodation, stating it will disrupt instruction. What is the school's obligation?
A: The school must support the teacher's decision because classroom management takes priority.
B: The school must ensure the accommodation is implemented, as refusing violates the student's Section 504 rights
C: The school can permit the accommodation only if it doesn't interfere with instruction.
D: The student should be referred for IDEA evaluation instead because 504 doesn't adequately address anxiety.
CORRECT_Explanation: Once a student has a 504 plan with documented accommodations, the school must implement them across all settings, including general education classrooms. A brief break to manage anxiety is a reasonable accommodation that does not fundamentally alter instruction and directly supports access to learning. The teacher cannot unilaterally refuse. Option A is incorrect because individual teachers cannot override 504 plans. Option C falsely suggests the school has discretion to deny accommodations; 504 requires implementation. Option D is incorrect because a 504 plan is appropriate for managing accommodations related to anxiety; IDEA evaluation is not the required response.

question_stem: A school district's 504 coordinator learns that a student has been placed in a separate classroom away from nondisabled peers due to her disability. The school justifies this because the student "needs" a separate setting. Under Section 504, what is the appropriate response?
A: The placement is permissible because the district determined it is necessary.
B: The placement violates the principle that students with disabilities should be educated in the least restrictive environment
C: Section 504 does not address placement; only IDEA requires LRE.
D: The student should remain in the separate setting because that is what the parents requested.
CORRECT_Explanation: Both Section 504 and IDEA require that students with disabilities be educated in the least restrictive environment. General education placement is presumed appropriate unless services cannot effectively be provided there. Simply asserting "need" is insufficient; the district must justify separation. Option A is incorrect because Section 504 contains LRE principles that constrain district discretion. Option C is incorrect; Section 504 explicitly addresses LRE. Option D is incorrect because parent preference alone does not override the legal requirement for LRE; the team must determine whether general education is viable.

question_stem: A parent of a 504-eligible student requests that the school provide a one-to-one aide to help her child stay on task during instruction. The school responds that this is not a "reasonable accommodation" because it is too costly. According to Section 504, what is correct?
A: The school can deny any accommodation it deems too costly or burdensome.
B: Cost alone is not sufficient justification for denying an accommodation
C: Section 504 does not address the cost of accommodations; schools are never obligated to fund them.
D: All accommodations, regardless of cost, must be provided if a parent requests them.
CORRECT_Explanation: Under Section 504 and ADA, schools cannot simply deny accommodations based on cost. Cost is a factor in determining "undue financial burden," but schools must make a robust analysis and pursue reasonable alternatives. A one-to-one aide may or may not be required depending on whether less restrictive accommodations exist that would provide appropriate access. Option A overstates schools' discretion. Option C is incorrect; schools must fund accommodations. Option D is incorrect; the accommodation must be reasonable and necessary.

question_stem: A student with Section 504 eligibility is transitioning to high school. What does the school need to consider regarding the student's rights under the ADA in a postsecondary setting?
A: Section 504 and IDEA protections automatically carry over to college, requiring no additional planning.
B: The student should understand that postsecondary institutions have similar but not identical responsibilities
C: Postsecondary institutions have no obligation to serve students with disabilities.
D: The student will automatically lose all protections upon graduation.
CORRECT_Explanation: A key transition consideration is that college students must self-advocate and self-disclose their disabilities; colleges will not proactively identify or accommodate (unlike K-12 schools' obligation to locate and identify). Colleges must provide reasonable accommodations but not specialized instruction. The shift from school responsibility to student responsibility is dramatic and requires explicit transition planning and student education. Option A is incorrect because the obligations differ at postsecondary level. Option C is incorrect; ADA applies to postsecondary institutions. Option D is incorrect because protections continue but with different mechanisms.

question_stem: In developing a 504 plan for a student with a recently diagnosed anxiety disorder, the team should avoid which approach?
A: Including the parent in the development process.
B: Assessing the specific functional limitations in major life activities caused by the disorder.
C: Creating accommodations based solely on the medical diagnosis without assessing actual functional impact.
D: Documenting the plan in writing with specific accommodations.
CORRECT_Explanation: A 504 plan must be based on functional assessment—how does the disability actually affect this student's major life activities in the school context?—not simply on the diagnosis. Two students with "anxiety disorder" may have very different functional limitations and thus different accommodations. Option A is incorrect because parent participation is required. Option B is a best practice. Option D is required for a valid 504 plan. Option C represents a common error: assuming diagnosis predicts accommodation needs.

question_stem: A 504 plan is developed in September, and the student's needs are stable. In December, the student's condition improves significantly with medication, and the parent questions whether the plan is still necessary. What should the school do?
A: Automatically discontinue the 504 plan because the student's condition has improved and no longer meets the criteria for a disability under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act.
B: Periodically review the 504 plan to determine if the student still substantially limits major life activities, and adjust accommodations or discontinue the plan if the condition no longer substantially limits
C: Continue the 504 plan indefinitely without review because once a student is formally identified as having a disability and receives a 504 plan, the plan cannot be modified or discontinued under any circumstances.
D: Leave the decision entirely to the parent without conducting a formal evaluation or review to determine whether the student still substantially limits major life activities in one or more areas.
CORRECT_Explanation: Section 504 requires periodic review of plans. If a student's condition improves (due to medication, therapy, maturation, etc.) to the point where it no longer substantially limits major life activities, the school may discontinue the plan—but this is a significant decision requiring due process (notice and opportunity to be heard). Option A is incorrect because improved functioning requires evaluation, not automatic discontinuation. Option C is incorrect because plans must be reviewed periodically. Option D is incorrect because while parent input is essential, the school team makes the determination based on functional data.

question_stem: What is the primary distinction between a "504 plan" and a "health plan" in a school context?
A: Both documents are identical; they are merely different names for the same thing.
B: A 504 plan addresses disabilities that substantially limit major life activities and provides accommodations to ensure access to education
C: A health plan is more comprehensive and is preferred over 504 plans.
D: 504 plans address only physical disabilities; health plans address medical conditions.
CORRECT_Explanation: A critical confusion in schools is blending 504 plans with health/medical management plans. A student may need a health plan (tracking medication administration for diabetes) without needing a 504 plan (if diabetes doesn't substantially limit major life activities). Conversely, a student with a substantially limiting condition needs a 504 plan with accommodations. A diabetes management plan addresses medical care; a 504 plan addresses educational access. Option A is incorrect; they serve different functions. Options C and D misrepresent the scope of each document type.

question_stem: An athletic coach refuses to allow a student with a documented anxiety disorder to miss practice for a scheduled therapy appointment, stating that attendance is mandatory for team participation. What is the school's obligation under Section 504?
A: The school can enforce the practice requirement without modifications; disability accommodations do not apply to athletic programs, and students must choose between treatment and team participation.
B: The school must ensure that necessary mental health treatment is accommodated, even in athletic programs, by allowing the student to miss practice for therapy without losing athletic eligibility.
C: The student should be removed from the team entirely because of the documented anxiety disorder, as athletic participation is incompatible with mental health disabilities.
D: Section 504 protections apply only to academic classroom settings and do not extend to athletic programs, extracurricular activities, or other school-sponsored events.
CORRECT_Explanation: Section 504 and Title II of the ADA explicitly apply to athletic programs. A student cannot be penalized or excluded from athletics based on disability. If therapy is a necessary treatment supporting the student's ability to function, accommodating the schedule (allowing practice absences) is a reasonable accommodation that does not fundamentally alter the sport. Option A is incorrect because athletics are subject to Section 504. Option C is incorrect; disability does not preclude athletic participation. Option D is incorrect; Section 504 applies school-wide, including athletics.

question_stem: A school wants to implement a new district-wide bullying prevention program. A student with autism who is on a 504 plan experiences social anxiety that is substantially limited by the disability. What must the school consider in implementing this program?
A: The program should be implemented identically for all students; accommodations for 504 students are not permitted.
B: The program should be reviewed to ensure the student with autism can participate and benefit
C: The student should be excused from the program entirely.
D: The school should implement the program only for students without disabilities.
CORRECT_Explanation: All school programs and activities must be accessible to students with disabilities. When a student's 504-eligible disability creates barriers to participation in a school program, reasonable accommodations are required to ensure meaningful access. Accommodations might modify how the student participates but not eliminate participation. Option A incorrectly suggests accommodations cannot be considered for programs. Option C is too restrictive and contradicts the inclusion principle. Option D is discriminatory.

question_stem: A student qualifies for a Section 504 plan but refuses to use the accommodations (e.g., refusing extended time on tests despite being eligible). What should the school do?
A: Implement accommodations regardless of the student's preference, as they are required by law.
B: Discontinue the 504 plan immediately because the student has declined protections.
C: Ensure the student and parent understand the accommodations and their purpose
D: Allow the student to decline accommodations; once declined, the school has no further obligations.
CORRECT_Explanation: A mature student may choose not to use certain accommodations (e.g., a teenager may decline extended time in front of peers). However, the school should ensure informed consent, document preferences, and maintain the 504 plan. Student preferences change, and circumstances evolve. Declining one accommodation does not invalidate the entire plan. Option A overrides student autonomy. Option B is incorrect; declining an accommodation does not dissolve 504 status. Option D is incomplete; the plan's existence and periodic review remain important even if currently declined.

Document student preference in writing. If student refuses, document that refusal—it does not eliminate the school's obligation to offer. Use a real script: ''We have to offer this; you get to choose whether to use it. But understand what it does first.'' Follow up periodically.

question_stem: In training staff on Section 504 implementation, a school psychologist should emphasize that a critical difference from IDEA is:
A: Section 504 provides no services; it only addresses discrimination in hiring.
B: Section 504 is broader in eligibility than IDEA
C: Section 504 is more restrictive than IDEA and applies only to students with physical disabilities.
D: IDEA and 504 are completely separate systems with no overlap or coordination.
CORRECT_Explanation:
The essential distinction is scope: Section 504 casts a wider net for eligibility (broader "disability" definition under 504 than under IDEA's 13 categories), but provides less intensive services—accommodations rather than specialized instruction. Many students appropriately served under 504 would not qualify for IDEA's more intensive, federally-funded services. Option A is incorrect; Section 504 applies school-wide. Option C is incorrect; 504 covers physical and mental impairments. Option D is incorrect; the systems coordinate, and students may be eligible for both.

question_stem: The landmark case Brown v. Board of Education (1954) primarily established which principle regarding students with disabilities?
A: Parents have the right to place children in private schools at public expense.
B: All students, including those with disabilities, have a constitutional right to education in desegregated environments that is not inherently unequal.
C: Students with intellectual disabilities are not entitled to a public education.
D: Schools must provide the same curriculum to all students without modifications.
CORRECT_Explanation: While Brown explicitly addressed racial segregation, its foundational principle—that education is a fundamental right and segregation is inherently unequal—later informed disability rights cases. The decision established that segregation denies students equal educational opportunity. Option A is incorrect; Brown did not address private placement reimbursement. Option C is incorrect; later cases overturned the notion that students with disabilities could be excluded. Option D is incorrect; Brown addressed access and equality of opportunity, not curriculum uniformity.

question_stem: PARC v. Commonwealth of Pennsylvania (1971) is significant because it established:
A: That students with intellectual disabilities cannot receive education in public schools.
B: That students with intellectual disabilities have a right to a free public education and to education in the least restrictive environment.
C: That IQ testing is inherently biased and should never be used.
D: That parents can unilaterally place children in private schools.
CORRECT_Explanation: PARC was a landmark case affirming that students with intellectual disabilities (previously excluded from public education) have a constitutional right to education and that education must occur in the least restrictive environment possible. Option A is the opposite of what PARC established. Option C, while related to concerns about testing bias, was not PARC's holding. Option D is incorrect; PARC addressed the right to public education, not private placement authority.

question_stem: Mills v. Board of Education (1972) extended the principles of PARC by establishing that:
A: Only students with intellectual disabilities have a right to free, appropriate public education.
B: Students with all types of disabilities have a right to a free, appropriate public education and due process protections
C: Students with emotional/behavioral disabilities should be educated separately from other students.
D: Schools can exclude students if providing education would be too costly.
CORRECT_Explanation: Mills expanded beyond intellectual disabilities to encompass students with all disabilities, establishing that exclusion from public education is prohibited and that procedural due process is required for any discipline that might result in exclusion. Option A limits the scope incorrectly. Option C contradicts Mills' inclusion principle. Option D is incorrect; cost is not justification for exclusion.

question_stem: Larry P. v. Riles (1979) and PASE v. Hannon (1980) reached different conclusions about the use of IQ tests for special education placement of Black students. What was the key difference in their holdings?
A: Larry P. banned all IQ testing; PASE permitted all IQ testing without restriction.
B: Larry P. found IQ tests culturally biased and unsuitable for placement decisions for Black students
C: Larry P. applied only to California; PASE applied only to Illinois.
D: Both cases reached identical conclusions.
CORRECT_Explanation: These cases illustrate different judicial interpretations of the same issue. Larry P. (California) concluded that standardized IQ tests were culturally biased and could not be used for identification of Black students. PASE (Illinois) concluded that while tests could be misused, with proper comprehensive evaluation and safeguards, they were acceptable. Option A overstates both holdings. Option C is incorrect regarding their applicability. Option D is false; they reached opposite conclusions.

question_stem: The practical implication of these differing decisions (Larry P. vs. PASE) for school psychologists is:
A: School psychologists can use IQ tests in any manner they wish since the courts disagreed.
B: School psychologists must understand that IQ tests, while potentially useful, are not culturally neutral
C: School psychologists should not use any IQ tests because the courts questioned their validity.
D: IQ tests are only appropriate for White students and should be avoided for students of color.
CORRECT_Explanation: The legacy of both cases is that school psychologists should use tests as part of a comprehensive evaluation, interpret results carefully considering cultural factors, use multiple measures, and avoid over-reliance on IQ scores. Tests are tools that can be misused or used appropriately; comprehensive practice minimizes bias. Option A ignores the real concerns both cases raised. Option C is overly restrictive. Option D is explicitly discriminatory and contrary to professional standards.

question_stem: In Hendrick Hudson v. Rowley (1982), the Supreme Court established the standard for "FAPE" (Free, Appropriate Public Education). What is the Rowley standard?
A: Schools must provide the best possible education, maximizing student potential.
B: Schools must provide an education that is reasonably calculated to enable the child to receive some educational benefit
C: Schools must provide special education identical to general education, with no specialized modifications.
D: Schools must provide whatever parents request in an IEP.
CORRECT_Explanation: Rowley established a moderate standard: FAPE requires education that provides the child with some educational benefit (vs. no benefit or minimal benefit), not optimization or maximal benefit. This became the standard for evaluating whether a school's IEP is appropriate. Option A is incorrect; schools are not held to a maximization standard. Option C contradicts the purpose of special education. Option D is incorrect; appropriateness is determined by an IEP team, not parent preference alone.

question_stem: Endrew F. v. Douglas County School District (2017) was significant because it:
A: Reaffirmed the Rowley standard without any change.
B: Established a higher standard than Rowley: IEPs must be "reasonably calculated.
C: Eliminated the requirement for specialized instruction.
D: Made it easier for schools to provide minimal services.
CORRECT_Explanation: Endrew F. raised the bar from Rowley's "some educational benefit" to requiring progress that is appropriate given the student's age and abilities, with challenging, age-appropriate, ambitious goals. This represents a meaningful evolution in FAPE standards, requiring schools to provide more robust, meaningful educational progress. Option A is incorrect; Endrew F. explicitly addressed limitations of Rowley. Option C is incorrect; specialized instruction remains central to special education. Option D is incorrect; Endrew F. increases school obligations.

question_stem: Irving v. Tatro (1984) established an important principle regarding "related services." The case determined that:
A: Schools are not responsible for related services; parents must independently pay for and arrange all medical services needed for their child's education and access to school facilities.
B: Schools must provide services (like catheterization) that are necessary for a child to access education and benefit from instruction, even if the service is medical in nature, if it does not require a physician to perform.
C: Schools must provide all medical services without limitation, regardless of cost, complexity, or whether a licensed physician is required to perform the procedure.
D: Related services under IDEA apply exclusively to students with physical disabilities and are not available to students with other types of disabilities or educational needs.
CORRECT_Explanation: Tatro expanded the definition of related services to include health services necessary for school participation—specifically, catheterization—if it can be performed by a school nurse (not requiring a physician). This established that schools' obligations extend beyond academics to services that enable access and learning. Option A is incorrect; related services are school responsibility. Option C is incorrect; "medical services" performed by physicians are distinguishable from health services schools can provide. Option D is incorrect; related services apply broadly.

question_stem: Honig v. Doe (1988) is most directly relevant to which school psychology practice area?
A: Assessment and evaluation procedures used to identify disabilities and determine eligibility for special education services under IDEA.
B: Parent consent and notification requirements for evaluation, placement decisions, and changes to a student's individualized education program (IEP).
C: Discipline, specifically that students with disabilities cannot be disciplined for conduct arising from their disability without a manifestation determination, and there are limits on removals even for unrelated misbehavior ("stay-put" provision).
D: Least restrictive environment determination and the process for selecting appropriate educational placements that maximize inclusion with nondisabled peers.
CORRECT_Explanation: Honig v. Doe is the cornerstone of IDEA discipline law. It established the "stay-put" principle (student remains in current placement during disciplinary proceedings) and manifestation determination (school must determine if misbehavior is related to the disability before applying certain consequences). Option A, B, and D are not the primary focus of Honig; discipline is.

question_stem: A school proposes a 10-day suspension for a student with a disability who violated the conduct code by bringing a weapon to school. The student's IEP team must determine whether the conduct was a manifestation of the disability (per Honig v. Doe). Which factor is LEAST relevant to this determination?
A: Whether the IEP and services were appropriate and being implemented as written.
B: Whether the student had been recently evaluated and assessed for understanding of rules and consequences.
C: The student's race or ethnicity.
D: The relationship between the student's disability and the conduct.
CORRECT_Explanation: Manifestation determination focuses on whether the disability caused or substantially motivated the conduct. Relevant factors include: appropriateness of IEP and services, student's understanding of rules, disability-behavior relationship, and ability to control behavior. The student's race/ethnicity is not directly relevant to the manifestation analysis (though it may be relevant to broader equity concerns). Options A, B, and D are all factors in proper manifestation determination.

question_stem: Florence County School District Four v. Carter (1993) addressed parental placement in private schools. The case established that:
A: Parents can never be reimbursed for private school tuition under any circumstances; they must enroll their child in an appropriately funded public school program instead.
B: If a school fails to provide FAPE and the parent unilaterally enrolls the child in private school, the parents may seek reimbursement for private tuition IF the private school was appropriate and services were suitable, even if the school had not previously placed the student there.
C: Reimbursement is available only if parents obtained prior written agreement and approval from the school district before unilaterally enrolling the child in the private school.
D: Private schools must be accredited by a recognized accrediting body and meet state licensing requirements before parents can seek tuition reimbursement from the district.
CORRECT_Explanation: Carter is crucial because it allows parents to place children in private school without the district's agreement and still recover tuition costs if they can demonstrate the school failed to provide FAPE and the private placement was appropriate. This is different from cases requiring that the school have previously placed the student in that private setting. Option A is incorrect; reimbursement is available under specified conditions. Option C is incorrect; prior agreement is not required. Option D is incorrect; accreditation is not the standard; appropriateness is.

Use Honig v. Doe contrast: stay-put does not require pre-approval either.

question_stem: Cedar Rapids School District v. Garret F. (1999) significantly expanded schools' obligations for related services by determining that:
A: Schools are never responsible for any medical procedures or health-related services, regardless of whether they are necessary for student attendance.
B: Schools must provide health services like catheterization, suctioning, and ventilator monitoring during the school day if necessary for the student to attend school, even when expensive and technologically complex.
C: Parents must pay for all services and accommodations beyond basic classroom instruction, including any health-related or therapeutic interventions needed.
D: Only large school districts with substantial resources have legal obligations to provide related services and accommodations to students with disabilities.
CORRECT_Explanation: Cedar Rapids broadened Tatro by requiring schools to provide ongoing health supports (not just one-time services) even when they are intensive and costly. The school's inability to pay does not eliminate the obligation if services are necessary for school participation. Option A is incorrect; Cedar Rapids expands school responsibility. Option C is incorrect; schools fund related services. Option D is incorrect; all schools have the same obligations regardless of size.

question_stem: Schaffer v. Weast (2005) addressed a procedural issue critical to IEP disputes: who bears the burden of proof in due process hearings?
A: Always the school, regardless of who initiated the hearing.
B: Always the parents, regardless of who initiated the hearing.
C: The burden falls on the party challenging the school's IEP
D: Burden of proof is not used in special education disputes.
CORRECT_Explanation: Schaffer established that the party challenging a school's IEP proposal bears the burden of proof. This is significant because most due process hearings are initiated by parents, placing the burden on them to prove the school failed to provide FAPE. Option A is incorrect; burden depends on who challenges. Option B is incorrect for the same reason. Option D is incorrect; burden of proof is essential to due process.

question_stem: Forest Grove School District v. T.A. (2009) addressed whether parents can seek reimbursement for private school placement and services when:
A: The child was previously placed in private school by the district.
B: The school previously agreed to fund private placement, which is one contributing variable rather than a complete explanation.
C: The parents place the child in private school without prior district agreement or prior public school evaluation/placement, yet the school later fails to provide FAPE.
D: The private school is specifically approved by the district.
CORRECT_Explanation: Forest Grove extended reimbursement rights by allowing parents to recover costs even when they placed their child directly in private school without prior district-funded public services. The key requirement is that the school failed to provide FAPE and the private placement was appropriate. Prior public placement/evaluation is not required. Options A, B, and D describe scenarios that would support reimbursement but are not Forest Grove's specific holding.

question_stem: A school psychologist must testify in a due process hearing regarding whether the school provided FAPE. How is she likely to testify about the student's actual progress?
A: Actual progress is irrelevant; only whether the IEP process was followed matters.
B: Actual progress is one factor considered, but Rowley/Endrew F. standards do not require demonstrating actual achievement gains
C: The student must demonstrate significant achievement gains or the school failed.
D: Progress is documented in grades alone.
CORRECT_Explanation: A common misunderstanding is that case law requires proving the student made academic gains. Instead, Rowley and Endrew F. ask whether the IEP was reasonably calculated to provide appropriate benefit (given the student's abilities) and whether it was implemented. Lack of progress may suggest insufficient IEP, but the standard is one of appropriateness of process and plan, not guaranteed outcomes. Option A is too narrow; progress is relevant context. Option C sets an unrealistic standard. Option D ignores other measures of progress.
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must map the rule to the scenario facts and reject plausible alternatives that misuse the standard. Success depends on contextual judgment, not rote definition recall.

question_stem: In thinking about implications of case law for practice, a school psychologist should recognize that PARC, Mills, and Honig v. Doe collectively establish:
A: That students with disabilities must be segregated into separate facilities to ensure their safety and protection from mainstream students.
B: That schools have only minimal legal obligations to identify and provide services to students with disabilities in their districts.
C: That students with disabilities have the right to be educated in public schools in the least restrictive environment with procedural due process protections.
D: That parents retain complete and absolute authority over all educational decisions without any input from school professionals or due process procedures.
CORRECT_Explanation: These three cases form the foundation of IDEA: a right to education (PARC, Mills), location in least restrictive environment (PARC), and protection from discrimination in discipline (Honig). Option A contradicts inclusion principles. Option B contradicts the cases' holdings. Option D is incorrect; IEP teams make decisions, not parents unilaterally.
