question_stem: Case example: A school psychologist is asked to perform a focused assessment on a student suspected of having attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, combined type (ADHD-C). The psychologist already conducted a formal observation of the student in two classes, has administered a standardized broad-spectrum assessment, and interviewed the parents for developmental information. What other formal measure would you employ as part of your comprehensive evaluation?
A: Behavioral Assessment System for Children (BASC)
B: Working Memory Subtest from the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC)
C: Behavior Rating Inventory for Executive Function (BRIEF)
D: The Primrose attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) Test
CORRECT_Explanation: Although the BASC is an effective formal measure to aid in an ADHD assessment, it is considered a broad-spectrum assessment, which has already been administered. Answer B may be helpful if administered, but it will yield very narrow information. Answer D is a fictitious test and not valid. The only viable option is answer C because the BRIEF is correlated to several traits of ADHD. Remember, it is important and ethical to give the most current version of a standardized test. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the case scenario requires selection of the most appropriate executive function assessment from options including general behavioral rating scales.
construct_actually_tested: Executive function assessment tool selection: identification of BRIEF as targeted executive function screening versus general behavioral rating instruments
rationale: Although the BASC is an effective formal measure to aid in an ADHD assessment, it is considered a broad-spectrum assessment, which has already been administered. Answer B may be helpful if administered, but it will yield very narrow information. Answer D is a fictitious test and not valid. The only viable option is answer C because the BRIEF is correlated to several traits of ADHD. Remember, it is important and ethical to give the most current version of a standardized test. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed broad-spectrum behavioral assessment suffices for executive function concerns, conflating general behavioral measures with focused executive assessment.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Need for focused executive function assessment beyond broad-spectrum behavioral measures
distractor_misconception_B: The student confused working memory with behavioral executive function, misidentifying what constructs ADHD assessment requires.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Executive function versus working memory distinction in ADHD behavioral assessment
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed Primrose ADHD Test possesses equivalent validity and specificity to BRIEF, underestimating BRIEF's established evidence base for executive function assessment.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: BRIEF validity and specificity for ADHD executive function assessment
dominant_error_pattern: Executive function assessment is collapsed into broad-spectrum behavioral measurement; students fail to recognize that BRIEF is the targeted instrument for executive function deficits central to ADHD-C diagnosis.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (BASC only, redundant) is a step backward. Distractor B (working memory subtest) confuses cognitive working memory with behavioral executive function. Distractor D (Primrose ADHD Test) lacks BRIEF's evidence base and specificity. Teach: 'BRIEF measures planning, organization, inhibition, flexibility, emotional control—behavioral manifestations of executive dysfunction. BASC is general behavior; BRIEF is specific to executive function.' Ask: Does the student trouble plan homework, organize materials, control impulses, shift between tasks? BRIEF answers precisely. Show: WISC working memory is one cognitive component; BRIEF executive function is behavioral regulation. Frame: 'If ADHD-C is suspected and BASC is done, BRIEF adds the executive function specificity needed for intervention targeting.'

question_stem: Case example: A school psychologist is asked to perform a focused assessment on a student suspected of having attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, combined type (ADHD-C). The psychologist already conducted a formal observation of the student in two classes, has administered a standardized broad-spectrum assessment, and interviewed the parents for developmental information. In the situation provided, the Behavioral Assessment System for Children (BASC) teacher and parent forms show significant score variability. The teacher form reveals a T-score of 66, whereas the parent form illustrates a T-score of 78. How would you interpret these results?
A: While both forms show significant score differences, both sets of scores suggest the student has indications of an attention problem
B: Due to the significant score discrepancy, best practice is not to interpret the BASC results and put more emphasis on other measures to make a professional impression about attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)
C: Both sets of scores, while discrepant, are still within the normal (average) range and do not indicate clinical concerns
D: The teacher form does not indicate high-level attention concerns, but the parent form shows substantial concerns
CORRECT_Explanation: While both forms show significant score differences, both sets of scores suggest the student has indications of an attention problem On the BASC, T-scores above 65 are elevated and may signal a problem area for the stu- dent. Although there are significant score differences, both the parent and teacher forms are signifying that the student has difficulties with attention. In other words, both scores are concerning. Note that T-scores above 69 are "clinically significant." Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service Delivery
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the scenario requires interpretation of significant discrepancies between parent and teacher BASC forms and determines whether both scores remain interpretable.
construct_actually_tested: Cross-informant BASC score discrepancy interpretation and decision-making: application of findings when parent and teacher forms show substantial divergence
rationale: student has indications of an attention problem On the BASC, T-scores above 65 are elevated and may signal a problem area for the student. Although there are significant score differences, both the parent and teacher forms are signifying that the student has difficulties with attention. In other words, both scores are concerning. Note that T-scores above 69 are "clinically significant." Practices That
distractor_misconception_B: The student thought discrepancy warranted deemphasizing BASC results, misunderstanding that both elevated scores confirm problems.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Interpreting concordant elevated scores across raters despite absolute score differences
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed T-66 and T-78 fall within average/typical range, misunderstanding BASC-3 clinical significance thresholds where T≥65-70 indicates clinically elevated concern.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: BASC-3 T-score clinical elevation thresholds and range interpretation
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed T-66 indicates minimal concern while T-78 reflects clinical significance, misunderstanding that both scores represent clinically elevated and concordant behavioral problems.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Interpretation of concordant clinical elevation across different elevated T-scores
dominant_error_pattern: Cross-rater BASC-3 T-score discrepancy (teacher T-66, parent T-78) is interpreted as invalidating the assessment or as indicating differential clinical concern between raters, rather than recognizing that both scores exceed clinical thresholds and indicate concordant elevation.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors B (discrepancy warrants deemphasis) and D (teacher reports minimal concern) misread both scores. Both exceed clinical threshold (T-65–70 = clinical). Ask: Is T-66 above or below the cutoff? Above (borderline-to-clear clinical). Is T-78? Clearly above. Both raters report clinically elevated attention—does absolute-level discrepancy erase the problem? No; it shows consistent concern across settings. Frame: 'When teacher says T-66 and parent says T-78, both are saying attention problem. Yes, parent rates it more severe, but both agree: clinical concern.' Teach thresholds: T-65+ = clinical. Drill relentlessly.

question_stem: When collecting and analyzing data on student behavior, which three aspects of the target behavior should psychologists emphasize in their analysis?
A: Intensity, variability, and duration
B: Intensity, duration, and frequency
C: Duration, quantity, and quality
D: Duration, frequency, and quality
CORRECT_Explanation: In a functional behavioral analysis, psychologists typically examine a behavior in terms of intensity, frequency, and duration. All other responses have one term that is not a valid
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must recognize key measurement dimensions and their appropriate applications in comprehensive behavioral analysis, progress monitoring, and intervention evaluation.
construct_actually_tested: Behavior measurement targets: identifying frequency, duration, and latency as fundamental dimensions of behavior recording and analysis
rationale: In a functional behavioral analysis, psychologists typically examine a behavior in terms of intensity, frequency, and duration. All other responses have one term that is not a valid
distractor_misconception_A: The student replaced frequency with variability, confusing a core behavior dimension with a secondary data characteristic.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Core target behavior dimensions in functional analysis
distractor_misconception_C: The student chose quantity and quality as generic descriptors, not recognizing standardized analytic terms used in behavioral data collection.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Standard behavioral measurement terminology for FBA
distractor_misconception_D: The student substituted quality for intensity, indicating partial recall of the triad but incorrect technical label retrieval.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Technical term retrieval: intensity versus quality
dominant_error_pattern: Student replaced frequency (how often?) with variability (how consistent?) in the core behavioral measurement triad, missing that intensity, duration, and frequency are the standard analytic dimensions.
instructional_red_flags: Anchor the FBA measurement triad: Intensity (how forceful/severe?), Duration (how long does it last?), Frequency (how often does it occur?). Variability is a secondary characteristic of DATA, not a primary behavior dimension. Teach: 'These three dimensions let you describe WHEN and HOW MUCH a behavior occurs. Variability describes DATA CONSISTENCY, not behavior itself.'

question_stem: A fourth-grade student was referred to a school psychologist due to low academic performance. In addition to the Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) data and informal measures, the psychologist decides that a full cognitive test battery is appropriate to administer. At the initial special education meeting, the psychologist meets with the parents. What is best practice when describing the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC) results to the parents?
A: Start with interpreting the individual subtests, and then explain the global score
B: Start with interpreting the global score, and then explain major index scores
C: Only provide and interpret the global score
D: It is generally good practice only to describe areas of concern and below-average scores
CORRECT_Explanation: Test interpretation should begin from broad scores because these scores are the most valid and reliable. Item and subtest interpretation generally do not have the validity of broader scores. Answers C and D are not good choices because it is important to report the pattern of strengths and weaknesses. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service
complexity_rationale: This is Application because the student must choose the correct communication sequence for explaining WISC findings to parents based on psychometric interpretability hierarchy. It tests applied reporting practice.
construct_actually_tested: Parent-facing results communication protocol: global-score-first interpretation followed by major index pattern explanation
rationale: Test interpretation should begin from broad scores because these scores are the most valid and reliable. Item and subtest interpretation generally do not have the validity of broader scores. Answers C and D are not good choices because it is important to report the pattern of strengths and weaknesses. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed interpretation should begin with subtests, reversing the psychometric top-down sequence used for parent feedback.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Top-down score interpretation order in cognitive reporting
distractor_misconception_C: The student assumed only the global score should be interpreted, truncating needed explanation of major index-level strengths and weaknesses.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Balanced breadth-to-depth reporting of WISC results
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed reporting should focus only on deficits, ignoring strengths-based interpretation essential for comprehensive educational planning.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Strengths-and-needs integrative interpretation framework
dominant_error_pattern: Global WISC score interpretation is inverted to begin with subtests, violating hierarchical top-down reporting protocol required for effective parent communication.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A, C, D breach standard reporting order. Best practice to parents: (1) Explain global (full-scale) score, (2) Describe major index patterns, (3) Discuss relative strengths/weaknesses, (4) Connect to academic impact and recommendations. Starting with subtests overwhelms parents and violates psychometric hierarchy. Always lead with the most reliable score and zoom in progressively.

question_stem: Case example: Bullying and harassment are major problems in schools. Many times, administrators are required to have strict discipline codes to address bullying in their school. School psychologists play a pivotal role consulting with administrators and helping to mitigate bullying in schools. One of the first steps a school psychologist can take to address bullying issues in a school is to educate the administration about research related to this topic. A consulting school psychologist should emphasize which of the following concepts to a school's administration?
A: Ninety percent of students have experienced bullying in grade school
B: Suspending students accused of bullying may have a disparate impact on minority students and special populations
C: The school resource officer should be consulted on all bullying cases
D: Restorative practices, although effective for social and emotional issues, are not as effective for bullying cases
CORRECT_Explanation: Suspending students accused of bullying may have a disparate impact on minority students and special populations Many times, students accused of bullying are suspended from school, which is not nec- essarily an effective practice. Additionally, suspensions may negatively impact minority students and special populations, which is a social justice issue currently emphasized by the National Association of School Psychologists (NASP). An effective alternative to sus- pensions and to address bullying issues is restorative justice practices. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families, and Schools—Systems-Level Services and Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery

question_stem: Case example: Bullying and harassment are major problems in schools. Many times, administrators are required to have strict discipline codes to address bullying in their school. question_stem: Case example: Bullying and harassment are major problems in schools. Many times, administrators are required to have strict discipline codes to address bullying in their school. School psychologists play a pivotal role consulting with administrators and helping to mitigate bullying in schools. A student is caught harassing a group of students in a younger grade. A school psychologist is asked to intervene. What is an effective approach to this situation?
A: It is best to start individual counseling, but maintain student confidentiality
B: Seek parental permission and then start counseling
C: Recommend a restorative practice approach to the parents and administration
D: Recommend group interventions, such as group counseling, for the victims
CORRECT_Explanation: Although counseling is effective and may be part of a corrective measure, the bully should make amends with the victims in a positive manner. Restorative practices are a highly recommended and current intervention strategy. Direct and Indirect Services for
domain_name: Services to Promote Safe and Supportive Schools
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must evaluate bullying intervention approaches and select the strategy aligned with restorative and collaborative implementation principles throughout.
construct_actually_tested: Bullying intervention approach: restorative practice recommendation versus individual-focused counseling and group alternatives
rationale: Although counseling is effective and may be part of a corrective measure, the bully should make amends with the victims in a positive manner. Restorative practices are a highly recommended and current intervention strategy. Direct and Indirect Services for
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that maintaining confidentiality is the primary concern in interventions.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Restorative justice practices in school settings
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that parental permission is the first step before any intervention.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Parental consent requirements for school counseling
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that addressing the needs of the victims alone would resolve the bullying issue.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Restorative justice principles in school settings
dominant_error_pattern: School psychologists recommend individual counseling or group victim support as primary bullying interventions, overlooking the systemic restorative approach that requires harasser accountability, victim restoration, and community healing simultaneously.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A prioritizes individual confidentiality without addressing the group harm and community dysfunction that bullying creates. Choice B places parental permission as the *first* step when administrative and student safety concerns take precedence. Choice D focuses exclusively on victim support services while leaving the harasser's accountability and the peer environment unaddressed. Guide the distinction: Individual counseling is *complementary* but insufficient; restorative practice convenes all stakeholders (harasser, victims, community) to repair harm and rebuild safety. Share a restorative conference script.

question_stem: When initially entering into a mental health counseling situation with a student, a school psychologist needs to carefully explain the limitations of confidentiality. When is it r_igÂ± appropriate to breach confidentiality with a high school student?
A: When the student mentions that she has thoughts of scratching herself to relieve tension
B: When the student gives permission to breach confidentiality
C: When the student is thinking about fighting another student in the parking lot
D: When a student tells you she is gay
CORRECT_Explanation: Danger and safety to self or others are primary causes to breach confidentiality (answers A and C). Also, a student can give you permission to talk with others (answer B). When a student informs a mental health provider of their sexual preference, this is not considered a danger to self or others and confidentiality should be preserved. Direct and Indirect
domain_name: Mental and Behavioral Health Services and Interventions
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must map the rule to the scenario facts and reject plausible alternatives that misuse the standard. Success depends on contextual judgment, not rote definition recall.
construct_actually_tested: Structured ethical decision-making, competing-duty analysis, and defensible documentation; applied to 'When initially entering into a mental health counseling situation with a student' scenario discrimination
rationale: Danger and safety to self or others are primary causes to breach confidentiality (answers A and C). Also, a student can give you permission to talk with others (answer B). When a student informs a mental health provider of their sexual preference, this is not considered a danger to self or others and confidentiality should be preserved. Direct and Indirect
distractor_misconception_A: The student thought self-harm without imminent danger is a breach of confidentiality trigger.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Imminent danger threshold vs. all harmful behavior
distractor_misconception_B: The student assumed student permission alone is sufficient ethical justification to breach confidentiality.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Ethical vs. permitted confidentiality breach scenarios
distractor_misconception_C: The student thought planning a fight meets the threshold for confidentiality breach.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Imminent danger and actual threat vs. angry planning
dominant_error_pattern: Misidentifying the threshold for confidentiality breach by treating self-harm, anger, or LGBTQ+ identity disclosure as reportable matters rather than recognizing imminent danger as the standard.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing A, B, or C conflate distress with danger. Teach the breach standard: Imminent danger to self or others. A (self-scratching for coping) is self-harm, not imminent danger. B (permission alone doesn't justify breach without danger). C (fighting plan) is anger, not imminent danger. Option D (sexual identity disclosure) requires no breach—it's identity, not danger.

question_stem: Schools generally favor behavioral techniques based on B. F. Skinner's theories. Which one of the following statements best describes Skinner's beliefs?
A: Behavior is shaped by rewards
B: Behavior is influenced by a person's family environment
C: Behavior is shaped by consequences that immediately follow the behavior
D: Punishment is not an effective means to modify behavior
CORRECT_Explanation: Although all of the response choices are valid, answer C best describes Skinner's belief. Note that answer C is broader than the other choices and the word "consequences" covers both positive and negative reinforcements that may shape behavior. Foundations of
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student chooses the statement that best captures Skinnerian behaviorism among partially plausible options. The task requires precision about immediacy and consequence contingencies.
construct_actually_tested: Skinnerian operant principle that immediate consequences shape future behavior frequency across reinforcement types
rationale: Although all of the response choices are valid, answer C best describes Skinner's belief. Note that answer C is broader than the other choices and the word "consequences" covers both positive and negative reinforcements that may shape behavior. Foundations of
distractor_misconception_A: The student narrowed Skinner's position to rewards only, missing that consequences include both reinforcing and punishing contingencies that shape behavior.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Breadth of consequence-based operant conditioning
distractor_misconception_B: The student attributed Skinner to ecological family-system influence theories, confusing behaviorism with broader contextual-developmental perspectives.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Behaviorist theory versus ecological systems theory distinction
distractor_misconception_D: The student assumed Skinner rejected punishment effectiveness categorically, misrepresenting his broader consequence framework focused on measurable behavioral effects.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Skinner's operant framework beyond simplistic anti-punishment claims
dominant_error_pattern: Student narrowed Skinner's operant framework to rewards only, missing the broader principle that ALL consequences (reinforcing and punishing) shape future behavior frequency.
instructional_red_flags: Expand the statement: Skinner's behavior is shaped by CONSEQUENCES (plural), which include rewards AND punishments AND removal of aversives. Show 5 classroom examples spanning all consequence types. Use the phrase 'immediate consequences' repeatedly to tie to the core principle.

Which intervention strategy is being employed to help Jack build his empathy for others?
A: Behaviorism
B: Cognitive intervention
C: Authentic environmental intervention

question_stem: Case example: A school psychologist is working with a first-grade student named Bill. Bill has a moderate level of autism and he is also suspected of having attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). The psychologist's current task is to teach Bill a classroom routine that involves several transitions. During the intervention, the psychologist conducts a task analysis and reduces the correct behavior into multiple smaller steps. The psychologist follows a systematic instructional technique called discrete trial instruction, with several repeated trials in a highly structured environment. In this situation, which behavioral technique is employed?
A: Applied behavior analysis and intervention
B: Cognitive behavioral therapy
C: Discrete behavioral analysis and intervention
D: Discrete behavioral modification
CORRECT_Explanation: Applied behavior analysis (ABA) is an effective approach with students who have autism. ABA includes the key aspects of task analysis, a highly structured environment, and systematic means of teaching new skills. The other choices may contain valid terms, but the other choice options are not completely valid names of the correct method. Note that the question has extraneous information that acts as a distractor (e.g., ADHD). Direct

question_stem: School psychologists are consulted on crisis topics, plans, and interventions. Which one of the following statements is regarded as the best approach to crisis preparation?
A: Crisis interventions should address individuals, groups, and systems

question_stem: Students who have an external-locus-of-control perspective typically have difficulty in school and in life. An external locus of control is most closely aligned with which of the following?

question_stem: Case example: A school psychologist, practicing in an affluent public school district, is notified by the district's crisis team that an unfortunate situation has occurred. A seventh-grade student died from a self-inflicted injury late at night. The next morning, the psychologist is called to assist the crisis team at the student's school. The administration has already been notified of the situation and they are awaiting support from the district crisis team. In the situation just described, the initial and primary concern is which of the following?
A: You must support and provide resources for the student's family
B: You must locate the student's classmates within the school and provide them support
C: You must debrief all staff about the situation before school starts
D: You must discuss and plan for contagion effects with school staff
CORRECT_Explanation: Although it is important to talk to staff as soon as possible and provide support to the family and peers of the student, it is primary to plan for contagion effects and prevent dangerous copycat actions by others who are sensitive to suicidal topics. Typically, a discussion with key staff will yield names of sensitive students who should be monitored and supported. Note that answer C may not be practical because holding a meeting before school for all staff is very difficult. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families,

distractor_misconception_A: The student prioritized immediate family support as the school crisis team's first action after a student suicide, not knowing that planning for contagion effects among vulnerable current students is the initial primary concern before external family outreach.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Suicide contagion prevention priority versus family support timing in school crisis response
distractor_misconception_B: The student identified direct peer support as the primary first action after learning of a student suicide, not recognizing that contagion planning—identifying which students are vulnerable and how to reach them—must precede delivering that support.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Contagion risk identification priority before peer support delivery in suicide crisis response
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed debriefing all staff before school starts is the initial primary crisis response after a student suicide, not knowing that a full pre-school staff meeting is logistically impractical and that contagion planning with key staff takes priority.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Contagion planning with key staff versus full pre-school staff debrief as first response
dominant_error_pattern: When facing a newly disclosed student suicide, practitioners may prioritize immediate emotional support to classmates or informational briefing without first establishing the contagion risk identification and prevention planning that must precede peer outreach.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A (family support first) misdirects the school team's immediate priority; while important, family support follows the school's contagion planning among current students. Choice B (locate and support classmates immediately) skips the risk assessment and prevention planning step; students may want to help directly without realizing they need contagion strategy first. Choice C (staff debrief before school) assumes time unavailable in typical crisis response; students may believe all staff need briefing before students return without recognizing that only key staff need pre-school contagion planning. Teach: Use the case to walk through ordering: 1) Identify vulnerable students (friend groups, known suicidal ideation history), 2) Plan contagion prevention messages and support structure, 3) Then deploy peer support; practice identifying who needs what information in what sequence.

A seventh-grade student died from a self-inflicted injury late at night. The next morning, the psychologist is called to assist the crisis team at the student's school. The administration has already been notified of the situation and they are awaiting support from the district crisis team. Which one of the following postvention intervention strategies is valid and effective?
A: Provide students with verifiable facts and several details about the suicide to mitigate rumors
B: It is important to make a special early-morning school-wide announcement to all students and staff to prevent rumors
C: With mental health staff on-site, a special school-wide assembly should be conducted ' to address contagion factors
D: Provide in-school resources and active counseling spaces for students who need additional support
CORRECT_Explanation: Provide in-school resources and active counseling spaces for students who need additional support Answer A may seem like a good answer; however, too many details about a suicide are not necessarily a good idea, especially for younger students. distractor_misconception_B: The student believed a general school-wide morning announcement about a student suicide is appropriate and prevents rumors, not knowing that announcing details to the full school population is not standard practice and can amplify contagion risk in large schools.

instructional_red_flags: Choice A (verifiable facts and details) violates contagion research by sharing method information; students may believe transparency about suicide mechanisms helps counter rumors without understanding that method detail elevation is a primary Werther effect driver. question_stem: A school psychologist conducts an evaluation for a gifted high school student. During the interview, the student is found to be highly opinionated and states that she wants to follow a defined occupational path as a criminal lawyer. The student thinks criminal lawyers have a socially desirable image. According to Erik Erikson, this student is navigating which stage of development?
A: Adolescent versus preadolescent stage
B: Initiative versus shame and guilt
C: Identity versus role confusion
D: Industry versus inferiority
CORRECT_Explanation: The typical age range for students within the "identity versus role confusion" stage is 13 to 18 years. Although the age range for stage theories is important to consider, it is most important to consider the aspects that define a particular stage. In the question, the gifted and talented description is not a significant consideration. It can be inferred that the student's occupational path provides a sense of identity. instructional_red_flags: Students choosing A, B, or D are missing the signature of identity exploration. Occupational aspiration, role idealization, and self-definition are core to the Identity vs. Role Confusion stage (adolescence, 12–18 years). Distinguish: Initiative is about planning/purpose (preschool); Industry is about task competence (elementary); Identity is about self-definition and future roles (adolescence). question_stem: A school psychologist is conducting a training for school staff on the topic of school violence. A staff member asks about the role of violent television shows and video games. The psychologist responds that children can learn aggressive behaviors by watching violent movies or by observing the actions of others. The psychologist's comments are based on studies conducted by which psychologic researcher?
A: Abraham Maslow
B: B. F. Skinner
C: Albert Bandura
D: Jean Piaget
CORRECT_Explanation: Albert Bandura believed in the cognitive component of learning and he believed that people can learn by watching others. His beliefs were in contrast to the principles of strict behaviorism. Bandura is also known for his famous Bobo doll study involving violence.

question_stem: Case example: A school psychologist is evaluating a sixth-grade male student named Cameron for special education services. The psychologist administers a full cognitive test battery (e.g., Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children [WISC]) and various socialemotional measures. The preliminary results are as follows: The full-scale standard score on Cameron's cognitive test battery is 113 (SS-113). All major cognitive domains (indexes) were found to be within two standard score points of each other. The Behavior Assessment System for Children (BASC) indicates that both the teacher and parent forms have T-scores above 69 within the attention domain. The semi-structured student interview reveals that Cameron follows the rules of the school, but he appears to follow these rules only to avoid punishment or gain rewards. Given the previous scenario, what preliminary statement can be made about Cameron's overall cognitive ability?
A: Cameron's standardized test score suggests that he might be gifted, but further information is needed to confirm this initial finding
B: Cameron's cognitive ability is considered well within the average range
C: Cameron's cognitive abilities are considered low average
D: Cameron's cognitive abilities are situated within the high-average to above-average range
CORRECT_Explanation: Cameron's cognitive abilities are situated within the high-average to above-average range Given a common standard error of measurement of +5 points, a full-scale standard score of 113 would situate the true score within the average to above-average range (108-118). Most major tests have a standard average score range of 85 to 115. Standard scores typi- cally near 110 are considered high average. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service Delivery
complexity_rationale: This question is Application because the student must classify an FSIQ of 113 within normative bands while considering profile consistency and SEM context. The answer requires quantitative interpretation in case formulation.
construct_actually_tested: Classification of full-scale cognitive scores into normative descriptive ranges using SEM-informed interpretation within case-based psychoeducational reporting
rationale: range Given a common standard error of measurement of +5 points, a full-scale standard score of 113 would situate the true score within the average to above-average range (108-118). Most major tests have a standard average score range of 85 to 115. Standard scores typically near 110 are considered high average. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service
distractor_misconception_A: The student assumed a score of 113 suggests giftedness, confusing high-average functioning with gifted-range cutoffs.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IQ classification bands: high average versus gifted
distractor_misconception_B: The student categorized 113 as average only, missing standard classification conventions that place this level in high-average to above-average territory.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Standard score descriptive classification conventions
distractor_misconception_C: The student labeled 113 as low average, indicating major miscalibration of normative score bands.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Norm-referenced IQ band calibration
dominant_error_pattern: Full-scale standard scores in the 113–115 range are misclassified as gifted or confused within average bounds, rather than correctly categorized as high-average to above-average range reflecting above-average functioning without gifted designation.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A, B, C misplace a score of 113. Using standard IQ classification: SS 113 is 0.87 SDs above mean (roughly 84th percentile), placing it solidly in the high-average to above-average range. This is not average (85–115 band center), not gifted (≥130), but clearly above typical. In case context, the psychologist should report: 'Cameron's cognitive ability is in the high-average to above-average range,' supporting normal-gifted screening but not exceptional services without additional evidence.

question_stem: Case example: A school psychologist is evaluating a sixth-grade male student named Cameron for special education services. The psychologist administers a full cognitive test battery (e.g., Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children [WISC]) and various socialemotional measures. The preliminary results are as follows: The full-scale standard score on Cameron's cognitive test battery is 113 (SS-113). All major cognitive domains (indexes) were found to be within two standard score points of each other. The Behavior Assessment System for Children (BASC) indicates that both the teacher and parent forms have T-scores above 69 within the attention domain. The semi-structured student interview reveals that Cameron follows the rules of the school, but he appears to follow these rules only to avoid punishment or gain rewards. Based on the provided Behavior Assessment System for Children (BASC) results, what is an accurate initial impression regarding Cameron's attention?
A: Cameron's attention scores are within the high-average range and indicate he might have attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)
B: Cameron's BASC T-scores place him in a category for significant attention problems, but more information is needed to confirm the presence of a disability
C: Cameron's scores on the BASC are only elevated slightly above normal, so it is prudent to monitor his level of attention throughout the school year
D: Cameron's scores on the BASC are well within the average range and both the teacher and parent forms confirm each other's observations in two environments
CORRECT_Explanation: A T-score of 69 or more is near the clinically significant range on the BASC. Although the two scores are cause for concern, more information from observations and other measures is necessary to make professional judgments regarding ADHD. The BASC is a good tool, but it is only one source of data and the National Association of School Psychologists (NASP) endorses multiple sources of information in formal evaluation
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the scenario requires interpretation of specific BASC T-score elevations within context of special education referral and determination of whether scores indicate significant problems.
construct_actually_tested: BASC T-score elevation severity interpretation in special education context: discrimination of clinically significant attention-related elevation from normal-range or marginal findings
rationale: A T-score of 69 or more is near the clinically significant range on the BASC. Although the two scores are cause for concern, more information from observations and other measures is necessary to make professional judgments regarding ADHD. The BASC is a good tool, but it is only one source of data and the National Association of School Psychologists (NASP) endorses multiple sources of information in formal evaluation
distractor_misconception_A: The student assumed T-69+ was high-average rather than clinically significant, misunderstanding BASC-3 cutoffs.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: BASC-3 T-score clinical elevation range versus high-average range distinction
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed T-69+ indicated only slight elevation, misunderstanding that T≥70 reflects clinically significant concern.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: BASC-3 T-score clinical significance threshold interpretation
distractor_misconception_D: The student treated T-scores of 69+ and concordant parent-teacher scores as average-range findings, fundamentally misinterpreting BASC-3 score elevation.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Recognition of BASC-3 T-score elevation as clinically significant not average-range
dominant_error_pattern: BASC-3 T-score elevation is misinterpreted as high-average range or marginal concern rather than recognized as clinically significant; students fail to differentiate clinical T-scores from normal variation despite average cognition and adequate academics.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (high-average), C (only slightly elevated), D (average range) all misread the metric. T-69+ is clearly clinical—not average, not marginal, not high-average. Ask: Show a T-score scale (mean 50, SD 10). Where does 69 fall? 1.9 SDs above mean—well into clinical range. Teach: 'BASC-3 uses 65–70 as the clinical threshold. Scores 69+ indicate clinically significant concerns.' Frame: 'Cameron's cognition is average and grades are adequate, but attention is a specific, clinically elevated concern. These coexist: intellectually capable + academically adequate + attention dysregulation = focused intervention on attention/executive function, not global disability.' Model accurate impression: 'Cameron's T-scores place him in the clinically significant range for attention. While his cognition is average and academics adequate, attention deficits warrant targeted intervention.' Drill: T-69+ = clinical concern.

question_stem: A school psychologist typically discusses with parents a wide variety of behavioral and emotional disorders listed in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed., text rev.; DSM-5-TR). In one particular case, the psychologist evaluated a student who had significant attention problems. Both formal and informal measures indicated that the student had difficulty sustaining his concentration and he had been observed to fidget across multiple domains. During the parent meeting, the psychologist mentioned to the parents that their son had characteristics of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). The parents wanted to know the research on ADHD. Which of the following choices is an appropriate research-based response about ADHD?
A: ADHD is diagnosed in approximately 17% of a school's population
B: ADHD is overdiagnosed, but the parents could benefit from a consultation with their pediatrician
C: School psychologists do not diagnose ADHD, but rather school psychologists identify students with attention difficulties
D: ADHD is diagnosed more in males than in females
CORRECT_Explanation: This question might seem simple at first, but some response options have a slight error that invalidates their selection. The prevalence of ADHD is approximately 3% to 7% of the population, not 17%. Although ADHD might be overdiagnosed, this statement is debatable and partly based in professional opinion rather than a solid research-based response. Answer C might be a valid response, but answer D is the best answer because it is supported by long-standing research. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families,
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the learner must understand the school psychologist's role in relation to clinical diagnosis and identify the correct statement about ADHD assessment in schools. Success requires professional role knowledge.
construct_actually_tested: Understanding school psychologist role in identification versus diagnosis of ADHD and related disorders

question_stem: A teacher alerts the school psychologist to a high school transgender student who has made suicidal comments during a class assignment. The psychologist meets with the student and decides that a full suicide assessment is prudent. Given this situation, it is critical to do which of the following?
A: Secure parent permission immediately and start a student clinical interview
B: Inform your school administrator that you are engaged in a priority assessment
C: Do not leave the student unsupervised during any part of the process
D: Immediately notify the parent(s) and conduct a suicide assessment with another mental health team member
CORRECT_Explanation: Although it is important to notify parents and your school administrator of the assessment (answers A and B), it is most critical that you never leave a potentially suicidal student alone. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families, and Schools-Student -Level

Success requires discriminating Do not leave the student unsupervised during any part of the process from plausible but mismatched alternatives like Secure parent permission immediately and start a student clinical interview.

distractor_skill_deficit_A: Suicide assessment emergency protocol overriding normal consent requirements for safety
distractor_misconception_B: The student thought that informing administration has priority over ensuring the student remains supervised during assessment.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Suicide assessment safety protocol prioritizing continuous observation over notification sequencing
distractor_misconception_D: The student assumed that parent notification and assessment can occur with only single mental health professional presence.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Suicide assessment safety protocol requiring paired monitoring and dual supervision
dominant_error_pattern: Learners subordinate continuous student supervision to sequential procedural tasks (securing parent permission, informing administrators), failing to recognize that suicide assessment safety protocol prioritizes never leaving a suicidal student unattended.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A requires parent permission before conducting assessment (blocks immediate safety action). Choice B prioritizes administrator notification (procedural notification over immediate physical safety). Choice D assumes single-clinician assessment is acceptable. Misconception: 'We need to do paperwork and permissions before we make sure they're safe.' Teach suicide assessment *safety protocol*: (1) *Do not leave the student alone* at any point (while assessing, while waiting for parent contact, while arranging referral). (2) Maintain continuous line-of-sight observation. (3) Assign second staff member to co-monitor if assessment is prolonged. (4) *Parallel actions*: while keeping student supervised, notify parents, document findings, arrange hospitalization/referral. Parent permission is desired but does not override immediate safety supervision.

question_stem: Prior written notice must be given to parents for which of the following?
A: When a special education student is assessed for suicide
B: A change of service or educational programming
C: When a special education student is suspended
D: When a special education student is assessed for a threat
CORRECT_Explanation: If a special education student has any formal change of service or programming, the school must secure parent approval before changes are made. Note that any student can be assessed for suicide or threat without formal consent due to extreme safety concerns.

question_stem: Deb was referred to a school's special education team for a full evaluation. The referral centered on Deb's below-average performance in reading and her lack of an age-expected response to reading interventions. While reviewing academic records and Deb's performance on the Woodcock-Johnson Test of Achievement, the school psychologist notices that Deb struggles with quantitative concepts as well as reading. In addition to conducting a reading disorder evaluation, it would be appropriate to screen Deb for which specific disorder?
A: Dyslexia
B: Dyscalculia
C: Dysgraphia
D: Specific learning disability
CORRECT_Explanation: In this scenario, Deb is already being assessed for dyslexia (reading disorder) due to the formal referral related to her poor reading. The new information indicates that she may also have a quantitative reasoning problem, which is the hallmark feature of dyscakulia. Note that dysgraphia is related to writing. Related to this case, the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed.; DSM-5) identifies the learning disorder with classifiers under the umbrella term "Specific Learning Disorder With Impairments

question_stem: What is the primary difference between punishment and negative reinforcement?
A: Punishment increases a desired behavior by decreasing undesired behavior, whereas negative reinforcement does not
B: Negative reinforcement increases behavior, whereas punishment decreases behavior
C: Negative reinforcement decreases desired behavior, whereas punishment decreases undesired behavior
D: Punishment and negative reinforcement are functionally the same terms
CORRECT_Explanation: Negative reinforcement increases behavior, whereas punishment decreases behavior Negative reinforcement can increase a target behavior and is typically confused with the term "punishment" because punishment also uses negative means to achieve its goal. On the other hand, punishment uses negative means to decrease a behavior (e.g., pun- ishing a child to decrease stealing). To help with the distinction between terms, think of the annoying seat-belt reminder noise in a car. The noise is a negative stimulus designed to increase your seat-belt-buckling behavior. Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery
domain_name: Mental and Behavioral Health Services and Interventions
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the primary conceptual difference between two often-confused operant terms. No scenario analysis is required beyond retrieving directional effects on behavior.

Together: Negative reinforcement increases behavior by removing an aversive. Use escape-avoidance examples: removing seat-belt buzz increases buckling up. Drill with decision tree: removal → behavior increases = negative reinforcement.
primarySnippet: Negative reinforcement increases behavior by removing an aversive stimulus, whereas punishment decreases behavior by adding an aversive consequence or removing a positive one.

question_stem: Which of the following behavioral reinforcement schedules has shown to be effective, but it is most difficult to modify the behavior once established using this technique?
A: Fixed-ratio reinforcement
B: Mixed-ratio reinforcement
C: Variable-ratio reinforcement
D: Fixed-interval reinforcement
CORRECT_Explanation: Variable reinforcement schedules are effective because the participants do not know when they will be rewarded. Once established, the participants are likely to continue the behavior because they do not know when the specific behavior will be reinforced, so they will continue the behavior in the hope that each behavior will result in a reward. Variable reinforcement behaviors are difficult to extinguish once established. Foundations
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because students must select the schedule based on its behavioral persistence profile, not just naming schedules. It requires applying extinction-resistance properties to schedule comparison.
construct_actually_tested: Variable-ratio schedule properties: high response rates and strong resistance to extinction after conditioning
rationale: Variable reinforcement schedules are effective because the participants do not know when they will be rewarded. Once established, the participants are likely to continue the behavior because they do not know when the specific behavior will be reinforced, so they will continue the behavior in the hope that each behavior will result in a reward. Variable reinforcement behaviors are difficult to extinguish once established. Foundations

distractor_skill_deficit_A: Reinforcement schedule effects on extinction resistance

distractor_skill_deficit_B: Canonical operant schedule taxonomy

instructional_red_flags: Choice A incorrectly identifies MTSS/RTI as inequitable (they are prevention frameworks). Choice B assumes special education inclusion is inequitable (inclusion itself is not; unequal *access* to quality inclusion is). Choice C assumes 504 plans create inequity (504 is legal accommodation; disproportionate *underidentification* of students who need it is inequitable). Misconception: 'MTSS and RTI cause disproportionality' or 'Section 504 is unfair.' Teach: These are structures/tools. *Disproportionality* occurs when (1) referral patterns are unequal across racial groups, (2) eligibility decisions apply different thresholds by race, or (3) quality of services varies by race. Example: RTI itself is neutral; if Black students are over-referred to special education *via* RTI without equal opportunity for Tier 1/2 success, the tool amplifies disproportionality. Analyze *where in the process* inequality enters, not whether the framework exists.

question_stem: Case example: Michele is a student engaged in a school's response-tointervention (RTI) process for reading comprehension issues. When she started the RTI process, her baseline score was 3, which was considered below that of her same-aged peers. She is given weekly assessments to gauge her progress with reading comprehension. Michele's scores are based on a 1 to 10 scale, with 10 being the highest and most positive score possible. Her scores for the past several weeks are 1, 5, 7, 7, and 10. What is the mean score in the given set of numbers?
CORRECT_Explanation: The mean, or average, is found by adding all the numbers in a set of numbers, and then dividing by the total numbers given in the set. In the case provided, the sum of the set is 30. There are five numbers given. Dividing 30 by 5 equals 6. Practices That Permeate All
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the learner must calculate change or interpret data from a case example involving Michele's RTI progress, requiring numerical reasoning about intervention outcomes.
construct_actually_tested: Numerical computation and interpretation of student achievement data within response-to-intervention progress monitoring

distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that the highest score should be the mean.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Calculating the arithmetic mean
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that the mean is calculated by averaging only the middle scores.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: calculating the mean of a data set
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that the mean is calculated by selecting the middle value in a sorted list.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Calculating the mean of a data set
dominant_error_pattern: Students apply incorrect procedures to the mean calculation, either selecting the highest data point, averaging only central scores, or confusing the median (middle value when sorted) with the arithmetic mean (sum divided by count).
instructional_red_flags: A treats the maximum value (10) as the mean. B averages only the middle two values (5 + 7)/2 = 6, then selects 5.5 as a midpoint error. C applies median procedure (sorts 1, 5, 7, 7, 10; selects middle = 7) instead of summing all—show step-by-step: (1 + 5 + 7 + 7 + 10) ÷ 5 = 30 ÷ 5 = 6.0. Emphasize: mean includes all values; median is the middle value. Both are used in RTI but answer different questions.

question_stem: A school psychologist is asked to evaluate a new cognitive abilities screening test for a middle school. In the technical manual, the psychologist notices the full-scale standard score of the new test has a statistical correlation of 0.68 with another well-respected cognitive test's full-scale score. Given these details, you can make which of the following assumptions?
A: The new test has acceptable reliability
B: The new test has unacceptable reliability
C: The new test shows a moderate effect size
D: The new test shows at least moderate validity
CORRECT_Explanation: The first two choices are not valid responses to this question because reliability involves the consistency of scores and performance across time. The correct answer involves the concept of validity One method of determining validity is found by comparing a test to another test that measures the same underlying trait. It is important to understand the difference between validity and reliability Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service

instructional_red_flags: Choice A conflates correlation with other test as evidence of reliability—students mistake convergent validity correlation for a reliability coefficient. Choice B reverses the strength interpretation—students read 0.68 as indicating poor reliability when that same value indicates moderate validity. Choice C misnames correlation as effect size—students confuse the two statistical properties (correlation is association; effect size is practical magnitude). The question deliberately omits reliability data. A 0.68 correlation with a respected test is convergent validity (moderate), not reliability. Teach the distinction: 'Reliability = consistency (test-retest, internal consistency, inter-rater). Validity = accuracy to construct (convergent, discriminant, predictive, content). question_stem: Trauma-informed care is an emerging area of practice that is emphasized by the National Association of School Psychologists (NASP). What are the four aspects of trauma-informed care?
A: Recognize, plan, react, monitor
B: Plan, intervene, respond, report
C: Realize, recognize, react, relate
D: Realize, recognize, respond, resist retraumatization
CORRECT_Explanation: This question might be difficult to answer if you are not well-versed in trauma-informed care and related concepts. NASP resources address this specific question and provide the four aspects of trauma care. The R alliteration may help test takers remember the four aspects. As mentioned, NASP is emphasizing trauma response and care; therefore, it is important to read NASP resources on this topic. Direct and Indirect Services for Children,

question_stem: A middle school administrator calls the school psychologist into a conference with a student's parents at the end of the school year. The student in question has been struggling this year and has failed many classes. On state assessments, the student has scored significantly below the standards for her grade level. The parents are worried about moving their daughter to high school because she does not have adequate academic skills. What would be a good recommendation for the school psychologist to make in this situation?
A: Administer the Light's Retention Scale to see whether the student qualifies for grade retention
B: Recommend remediation in core subject areas at the high school and allow the student to take a few elective courses
C: Seek additional information to determine whether other factors are involved in the student's struggles that may necessitate Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) or special education services
D: Recommend summer school to make up for failed courses instead of grade retention
CORRECT_Explanation: Seek additional information to determine whether other factors are involved in the student's struggles that may necessitate Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) or special education services Grade retention in general is not an endorsed practice. Although answer D is a good choice, it should be given after additional information is gathered on the problem. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families, and Schools—Student-Level Services
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because it requires the school psychologist to move beyond retention consideration and interpret the student's struggles as potentially requiring comprehensive MTSS assessment or special education evaluation.
construct_actually_tested: Decision-making logic to distinguish between grade placement versus comprehensive MTSS evaluation and special education eligibility assessment
rationale: student's struggles that may necessitate Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) or special education services Grade retention in general is not an endorsed practice. Although answer D is a good choice, it should be given after additional information is gathered on the problem. Direct
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that grade retention is determined by a specific scale.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Light's Retention Scale application criteria
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that remediation alone would address the underlying issues.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: MTSS comprehensive assessment process
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that summer school alone would address the underlying academic issues.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Comprehensive academic intervention planning
dominant_error_pattern: Students bypass comprehensive assessment by jumping to either a specific retention scale or a interim academic fix (summer remediation, elective-lightened schedule), missing the critical first step of investigating whether the student's struggles reflect gaps in foundational skills (MTSS Tier 2/3), a processing or learning disability (special education), or external barriers. This reflects premature solution-selection without problem definition.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A reverts to the Light's Retention Scale as a decision tool, misapplying a screening instrument as a diagnosis and ignoring that the scale is neither a retention determinant nor part of a comprehensive evaluation. Distractor B proposes high-school remediation with elective reduction, treating academic support as a placement fix without investigating whether the underlying cause is addressable within general education or whether special education eligibility is present. Distractor D recommends summer school, which is an intervention option but skips the essential prior question: Does the student need MTSS support (Tier 2/3 intensive intervention for skill gaps), special education (if a disability is present), or both? Teach: Use a diagnostic tree: Is the student's struggle due to (a) unmet Tier 1 instruction + lack of Tier 2/3 intervention? (b) an unidentified learning or processing disability? (c) social-emotional barriers? Gather data on each before choosing path. Show that summer school without diagnosis is a band-aid—the student may need year-round special education services or intensive MTSS, not a seasonal fix. Name the procedural error: Placement (grade level, program type) must follow eligibility determination and needs assessment, not precede them.
primarySnippet: The psychologist's role is to advocate for evidence-based interventions first and to document whether those interventions are working.

B: An increased incidence of suspensions and expulsions

CORRECT_Explanation: Answer D is the correct choice because zero-tolerance policies are not effective. All other choices are valid responses. Remember, the role of school is to educate students,

question_stem: Although token economies are effective systems to use in behavioral modification programs, what is a complaint often stated by teachers regarding these methods?
A: Token economies need relevant reinforcers to work effectively
B: Token economies are generally effective for younger elementary students, not older students
C: Token economies are sometimes cumbersome to implement and maintain
D: Token economies do not maintain the target behavior once withdrawn
CORRECT_Explanation: Token economies are effective in changing behaviors, but busy teachers have difficulty collecting data and rewarding students immediately after a desired behavior. Token economies are effective with all ages and are typically not too expensive to employ. If fading is done properly with a cognitive component, then the target behavior can be maintained after tokens are withdrawn. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families,
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the question asks for a common implementation complaint, requiring practical judgment about classroom feasibility rather than theoretical mechanics alone. Students must separate efficacy from usability barriers.
construct_actually_tested: Implementation-level limitation of token economies: logistical burden of tracking, immediate delivery, and ongoing maintenance

distractor_skill_deficit_A: Practical feasibility concerns in token economy implementation

distractor_skill_deficit_B: Developmental adaptability of token economy interventions

question_stem: A teacher who typically gives students large projects and assignments asks the school psychologist for help with two struggling students. The students are well behaved and do well in other classes, but they have difficulty completing work in her class. The psychologist's approach and recommendation should include which of the following?
A: An observation and task analysis to recommend how to break larger tasks into smaller steps
B: A time interval observation and recommendation to the school's Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) process
C: A review of academic records, followed by a consult with the parents
D: A narrative observation and recommendation to the school's response-to-intervention (RTI) process
CORRECT_Explanation: An observation and task analysis to recommend how to break larger tasks into smaller steps The key words in the question are "large projects." If students do not have problems in other classes and behavior is not a concern, then it should be suspected that the students have difficulty with long, complicated information or tasks. A task analysis is a critical component that is necessary to know where to break assignments into easier steps. A referral to the MTSS or RTI process is generally a valid response, but some intervention attempts are typically needed before a referral is made. A full intervention program, such as RTI, may not be appropriate in this situation if a simple fix will suffice. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families, and Schools—Student-Level Services

question_stem: A second-grade teacher asks a school psychologist to help with a student named Amy. Amy needs to develop her reading skills. The teacher stated that Amy is a fluent reader, but her comprehension is below that of her peers. Which comprehension reading strategy would the psychologist recommend?
A: Lindamood-Bell Reading Intervention
B: Survey, question, read, recite, and review (SQ3R)
C: Wilson Reading Remediation Program
D: Woodcock-Johnson Reading Intervention Program
CORRECT_Explanation: SQ3R stands for survey, question, read, recite, and review. This method asks the reader to activate their prior knowledge of the text and to create questions before reading. The previous steps increase metacognition and comprehension. Lindamood-Bell and Wilson programs (answers A and C) are well-known reading interventions but are not necessarily focused on reading comprehension as SQ3R is. Direct and Indirect Services for Children,

question_stem: What is an example of an accommodation for a student who struggles with reading fluency?
A: Having the student point to pictures of answers instead of writing answers about an assigned reading text
B: Having the student take a reading test that is more aligned with their level of reading
C: Allowing extra time for the student to read assignments or tests
D: Allowing the student to read books at their level of cognitive ability
CORRECT_Explanation: It is important to know the difference between an accommodation and remediation. Remediation typically changes the task or level of curriculum, whereas an accommodation changes the environment and/or conditions under which the task is completed. However, there is some debate about the exact nature of these concepts. Allowing for extra time does not change the academic task to be completed, but rather it accommodates for the time in which it must be finished. If you allow a student to read a lower level passage, this intervention changes the task that is required to be completed by other students. Answer A might be construed as a writing intervention, not a reading accommodation. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families, and Schools-Student -Level
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must identify extra time as appropriate accommodation addressing reading fluency as a barrier to test performance and access.
construct_actually_tested: Recognizing extended time as accommodation supporting reading fluency barrier removal during assignment and test completion
rationale: It is important to know the difference between an accommodation and remediation. Remediation typically changes the task or level of curriculum, whereas an accommodation changes the environment and/or conditions under which the task is completed. However, there is some debate about the exact nature of these concepts. Allowing for extra time does not change the academic task to be completed, but rather it accommodates for the time in which it must be finished. If you allow a student to read a lower level passage, this intervention changes the task that is required to be completed by other students. distractor_skill_deficit_A: Understanding accommodations for reading fluency
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that modifying the test level is an accommodation for fluency.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Understanding accommodations versus modifications
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that matching reading material to cognitive ability is an accommodation for reading fluency.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Differentiation between cognitive ability and reading fluency accommodations
dominant_error_pattern: Each wrong answer changes the content or level of what is being read or assessed — substituting picture-pointing for written response (option A), lowering the reading level of the test (option B), or matching reading materials to cognitive ability level (option D) — rather than addressing the fluency-specific time-pressure barrier while preserving the grade-level reading expectation. The dominant error is model-conflation: students select changes to what is read rather than to the conditions under which reading occurs, conflating reading content modifications with reading fluency accommodations.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing option A have selected a task-substitution that removes the reading component entirely rather than accommodating the fluency barrier — picture-pointing is not an accommodation for reading fluency but an exemption from reading. Students choosing option B are applying a reading-level modification when a condition-of-access accommodation is what the question describes; lowering the test level changes the standard, not the time available to meet it. Students choosing option D conflate reading fluency (rate and automaticity) with cognitive ability level — a student can have strong cognitive ability and a significant fluency impairment; matching material to 'cognitive ability level' may under-challenge the student or mismatch the intervention to the actual barrier. Teaching move: have students distinguish the three distinct reading constructs — comprehension, fluency, and decoding — and match each to its corresponding accommodation versus modification strategy.

question_stem: According to cognitive behavioral theorists, learning is supported by mental representations of new concepts merging with a person's existing mental concepts. In this example, existing mental concepts are called:
A: Visualizations
B: Neurocognitive representations
C: Imprints
D: Schemata
CORRECT_Explanation: This answer is straightforward and the term "schemata" (the plural of "schema") is well-known to learning specialists. "Schema" is defined by some professionals as the mental structure of ideas and a framework representing some aspect of the world. A schema can be a system of organizing and perceiving new information. All other choices

construct_actually_tested: Retrieval of PBIS universal tier goal establishing positive predictable school wide behavioral environment

instructional_red_flags: Teach schema/schemata as the foundational CBT construct: existing mental patterns or frameworks that organize experience. question_stem: When engaged in a professional consultation situation, which of the following personality traits are important elements for success?
A: Empathetic and trustworthy
B: Assertive and knowledgeable
C: Reliable and kind
D: Efficient and respectful
CORRECT_Explanation: Although all the other choices may be valid, the best choice would be answer A. The key words in the question are "personality traits." As Carl Rogers states, the ability to develop trust in both counseling and consultation situations is the critical foundation for success. Note that being knowledgeable is important (answer B), but the trait of assertiveness is a hallmark trait related to consultation. Practices That Permeate All Aspects of
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the learner must judge which trait set best predicts consultation success in professional contexts. It requires prioritizing foundational alliance variables among plausible options.
construct_actually_tested: Consultation relationship efficacy predictors: empathic stance and trustworthiness as primary traits for productive professional collaboration

distractor_skill_deficit_B: Personal relational traits (empathy, trustworthiness) versus professional competence characteristics
distractor_misconception_C: The student assumed reliability and kindness capture the essential personality foundation for consultation trust.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Empathy and trustworthiness personality traits versus behavioral reliability and kindness
distractor_misconception_D: The student confused professional competence traits with core personality foundations for developing consultation trust.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Personality traits supporting trust foundation versus professional competence characteristics
dominant_error_pattern: Students conflate professional competence traits (assertiveness, knowledge, efficiency) with the personal relational traits (empathy, trustworthiness) that are the psychological foundation for consultation safety and alliance.

question_stem: A psychologist is asked to collaborate and consult on a student with behavioral problems in the classroom. What are the basic steps of the behavioral model of consultation?
A: Consult with stakeholders, create a plan, implement the plan, and evaluate the plan
B: Consult with stakeholders, create a plan, implement the plan, and monitor the plan
C: Identify the problem, implement a plan, monitor the plan, and evaluate the plan
D: Identify the problem, consult with stakeholders, implement a plan, and evaluate the plan
CORRECT_Explanation: This is one of those seemingly simple questions that takes time to answer. Although all choices may seem valid, it is critical to identify and define the problem first. A problem must be circumscribed to better focus on the consultation conversation and interventions within a behavioral plan. The first two choices do not contain the term "identification" and therefore are not appropriate responses. Answer D is close, but it does not have the critical "monitor" phase of the model. For the exam, it is important to be familiar with behavioral models, mental health models, instructional models, and organizational models of consultation. As mentioned previously, models that focus on building the consultee's skills (indirect consultee-centered model) is a highly endorsed practice. Practices
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the task is selecting the correct ordered steps in the behavioral consultation model. The student applies procedural sequence knowledge to distinguish near-correct options.
construct_actually_tested: Behavioral consultation procedural sequence mastery: problem identification followed by plan implementation, monitoring, and evaluation

The school psychologist in this example should be aware of factors that might diminish the effectiveness of the consultation process. question_stem: When is it legal to disclose the confidential records of a student to a third party (e.g., outside the school)?
A: When a medical doctor requests student records and a student health issue exists
B: When your school administrator directs you to disclose confidential records
C: When you are ordered to do so by a police officer
D: When there is a safety issue involved
CORRECT_Explanation: The first two choices are invalid as the student's parents or guardian must give permission to disclose confidential records as indicated in the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA). Note that in answer A, a health issue could be mild and not serious. If a health issue is life-threatening, then it might be appropriate to disclose student information. Answer C is a more difficult consideration, but a police officer cannot make a psychologist disclose confidential information that may not be pertinent to a situation at hand. Only a judge (not law enforcement) can order the release of student records without parent permission, unless there is an immediate danger issue. Answer D involves safety and harm, which always trumps confidentiality laws. Foundations of
