question_stem: A school psychologist works as a private practitioner and is also employed by a large suburban public school district. A portion of this school district is very wealthy. During the summer months when the schools are closed, a parent asks the psychologist to complete a full cognitive assessment for her child, who attends a school that is different from where the psychologist works. The parent needs a full-scale cognitive test score so she can register her son at a private school. The parent is willing to pay the psychologist for the service. What should the school psychologist do in this situation?
A: A school psychologist can refer the parent to the school district's special education coordinator
B: A school psychologist can accept this specific type of work if they are licensed and qualified to do so as a private practitioner
C: A school psychologist can inform the parent that the service she seeks is provided free of charge by the public school district her son attends
D: A school psychologist can never accept this type of work
CORRECT_Explanation: If the psychologist in this situation is qualified and licensed to engage in private practice, then she can accept this type of work. One of the reasons this type of private practice work is ethical to accept is because public school districts are not required to test students who do not have suspected problems. If the student had a suspected disability, then the psychologist might not be able to accept this work because such issues are covered by public schools. School psychologists in private practice can test students for a variety of reasons, such as applications to a private school. question_stem: Catherine is a school psychologist who has been practicing for 3 years. She shares a job with another psychologist named Sean. Catherine recently discovered that Sean has been giving money to other staff for referrals to his private counseling practice. Many of the students Sean counsels for a fee are regular education students at the high school. What should Catherine do in this situation?

question_stem: Aversive behavior modification techniques and restraining are legal in some states. What is considered best practice regarding aversive techniques such as restraining?
A: It is best practice never to employ aversive techniques in any situation
B: Aversive techniques should only be employed when safety is an issue
C: Use aversive techniques if such techniques are endorsed by your administration and school district
D: It is best practice to use aversive techniques with elementary students
CORRECT_Explanation: Although aversive techniques should be avoided, there are some circumstances that involve safety in which such techniques are appropriate. As mentioned in this guide, safety issues trump several other constraints on professional practices, which makes other response options to this question poor choices; for example, a student is actively cutting himself in your office and you must physically restrain the child for his safety.

question_stem: A student who receives special education services brought a knife to school. The student claims that he forgot the pocket knife was in his backpack, which he had taken on a fishing trip with his father over the weekend. It is important to note that the student has been suspended for 2 days earlier this year for bullying issues. In the current situation, the student was suspended for 10 days. What violation of this student's rights occurred?
A: A special education student cannot be removed from school for more than 5 days, in total, without a review meeting
B: A special education student cannot be suspended for more than 7 consecutive days without a manifestation review
C: A special education student must have a special review meeting or manifestation meeting if suspended, in total, 10 days or more
D: Special education students cannot be expelled but they can be enrolled in another school that better addresses their needs
CORRECT_Explanation: A special education student must have a special review meeting or manifestation meeting if suspended, in total, 10 days or more Special education students can be suspended, but if the suspension totals more than 10 days, a review meeting must be held to determine whether the behavior plan is appro- priate and other needs of the student are being properly addressed. Note that answer B is not valid due to the number of days and the term "consecutive." Also, answer D is not accurate as special education student can be expelled in specific situations. question_stem: A student is receiving special education services for mild dyscalculia. The special education team leader has placed this student in a class designed for students with significant needs. The special education teacher reasons that it is better to "overserve" a student with mild needs than it is to underserve such students. In this case, which legal aspect of special education law does the teacher run the risk of violating?
A: Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA)
B: Least-restrictive environment (LRE)
C: Individuals With Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
D: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
CORRECT_Explanation: LRE is the specific legal clause within the IDEA which states that, "To the maximum extent appropriate, children with disabilities should be educated with children who are not disabled, and special classes, separate schooling, or other removal of children with disabilities from the regular educational environment should occur only when the nature or severity of the disability is such that education in regular classes with the use of supplementary aids and services cannot be achieved satisfactorily" (2004, Section 1412[a][5]).
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must identify which specific legal doctrine is implicated when a mildly disabled student is placed in an overly restrictive class. It requires clause-level law selection from competing statutes.
construct_actually_tested: LRE violation recognition within IDEA when placement is more restrictive than necessary for a student's documented needs
rationale: LRE is the specific legal clause within the IDEA which states that, "To the maximum extent appropriate, children with disabilities should be educated with children who are not disabled, and special classes, separate schooling, or other removal of children with disabilities from the regular educational environment should occur only when the nature or severity of the disability is such that education in regular classes with the use of supplementary aids and services cannot be achieved satisfactorily" (2004, Section 1412[a][5]).

distractor_skill_deficit_A: ESSA's focus on academic standards and accountability

dominant_error_pattern: Framing 'overservice' (more restrictive placement than needed) as good practice rather than recognizing it as a violation of the LRE mandate.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (ESSA), C (IDEA directly, when LRE is the specific principle), D (FERPA). LRE is a core IDEA principle requiring individualized placement decisions. Placing a student with mild dyscalculia in a self-contained class for students with significant needs violates LRE because the placement is more restrictive than necessary for that student. 'More is better' is NOT legal reasoning in special education; appropriateness and fit are. Teach: 'LRE violation = overplacement in restrictive setting. Even with good intentions, it breaks the law. Use supplementary aids first; restrict only if necessary.'

B: Section 504 is for students who have physical disabilities, not learning disabilities
C: Section 504 is for students who only have mental health disorders
D: Section 504 is a part of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, which prohibits discrimination based on disability, whereas IDEA is a separate law related to special education

IDEA and Section 504 serve different purposes: IDEA mandates a free, individualized special-education program (FAPE) with due-process protections and federal funding. Section 504 is a civil-rights law prohibiting discrimination based on disability in any federally funded program; it covers a broader population (physical, mental, emotional, developmental) but does not mandate free services — just access. A student may be ineligible for IDEA special education but still protected under 504. Teach: 'IDEA = special ed + free FAPE. 504 = no discrimination + accommodations (may or may not be free). Different laws, different purposes.'

question_stem: A parent of a sixth-grade student is suing a school district because the school psychologist tested the student 3 years back without parental consent. At the time of the evaluation, the psychologist called and left several messages for the parent asking her to provide consent for the child's special education review (reevaluation), but the parent never returned the calls. The calls were documented in the student's file. At a subsequent Individualized Education Program (IEP) annual meeting, the parent was concerned about the testing, but allowed services to continue for another 2 years. What is the most likely reason the parent's lawsuit will not succeed?
A: The parents were informed of their rights at the meeting 3 years ago
B: The student did not demonstrate harm from the services provided
C: Schools do not need parental consent to test for reevaluation meetings
D: The psychologist made documented attempts to contact the parents and the parents ultimately knew about the services but did not file a complaint at that time
CORRECT_Explanation: The psychologist made documented attempts to contact the parents and the parents ultimately knew about the services but did not file a complaint at that time As with many legal questions, the answer to this question can be debated. If a psychologist makes a documented "good-faith" effort to contact a parent for a student's reevaluation, then the psychologist may proceed cautiously if several parent contacts fail. In addition, the parent's complaint takes place 3 years after the event, but the law only provides a 2-year window to file a complaint. When answering legal or ethical questions, look for the best answer and be aware that some response choices will have elements of truth in them.
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must weigh documented good-faith contact attempts, delayed complaint timing, and parental acquiescence to infer litigation outcome. It requires procedural-facts-to-legal-risk reasoning.
construct_actually_tested: Procedural defensibility analysis in consent disputes: good-faith parent-contact documentation and complaint statute-of-limitations effects
rationale: ultimately knew about the services but did not file a complaint at that time As with many legal questions, the answer to this question can be debated. If a psychologist makes a documented "good-faith" effort to contact a parent for a student's reevaluation, then the psychologist may proceed cautiously if several parent contacts fail. In addition, the parent's complaint takes place 3 years after the event, but the law only provides a 2-year window to file a complaint. When answering legal or ethical questions, look for the best answer and be aware that some response choices will have elements of truth in them.
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that informing parents of their rights is sufficient for consent.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Parental consent requirements for special education evaluations
distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that lack of harm negates the need for parental consent.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Parental consent requirement for special education evaluations
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that parental consent is not required for reevaluations.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEA parental consent requirements for reevaluation
dominant_error_pattern: Underestimating the legal protection afforded by documented good-faith parent-contact attempts and statute-of-limitations rules for procedural violations; students confuse the absence of documented harm with the absence of a defense.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (informing parents of rights alone), B (lack of harm), C (no consent needed for reevaluation). Procedural safeguards are not rendered meaningless if a parent does not object. When a school makes documented attempts to contact parents, the parent receives notice of rights, and the parent participates in subsequent meetings without filing a complaint for a period of time, the legal burden shifts. This parent's delay (2 years of inaction after learning of testing) undermines the lawsuit. Schools document in writing: call logs, meeting notices, consent forms. Teach: 'Documentation of good faith + parent participation + delay in complaint = strong legal defense for the district.'

question_stem: According to the Individuals With Disabilities Education Improvement Act (IDEIA), how much time does a special education team have to complete a formal evaluation if signed permission to proceed with an evaluation has been provided?
A: 45 days
B: 60 days
C: 30 days
D: 90 days Case example for questions 97 and 98. A large suburban middle school has been having difficulty with reports of harassment directed toward students within the LGBTQIA+ community The administration asks the counseling department and the school psychologist to implement a systems-wide intervention to address this problem.
CORRECT_Explanation: The answer to this question is factual and straightforward. Once a parent signs permission to evaluate, the special education team has 60 days to complete the evaluation.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the fixed evaluation-completion deadline after signed consent under IDEIA. The answer is a direct statutory timeframe retrieval.
construct_actually_tested: IDEA/IDEIA formal evaluation completion deadline triggered by signed consent: 60-day compliance requirement
rationale: The answer to this question is factual and straightforward. Once a parent signs permission to evaluate, the special education team has 60 days to complete the evaluation.
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed the evaluation timeline was 45 days, overestimating the federal requirement and confusing it with a related procedural deadline.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: IDEIA evaluation timeline requirement
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed the evaluation timeline was 30 days, underestimating the federal 60-day requirement and likely confusing it with a shorter state or district timeline.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: IDEIA evaluation timeline requirement
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that the evaluation timeline is longer to allow for comprehensive assessments.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: IDEIA evaluation timeline requirement
dominant_error_pattern: Confusing state timelines with federal baseline; students apply varying state regulations without knowing the federal floor (60 days) that states cannot undercut significantly.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (45 days), C (30 days), D (90 days). IDEA/IDEIA federal requirement is 60 days from signed consent to complete initial evaluation, or the state's established timeline if shorter. Some states have 45-day timelines; few have longer. 30 days is too short; 90 days would require state authorization. Clarify: 'Federal baseline = 60 days. Some states shorter. No state can be longer without federal waiver. Know your state's timeline, but 60 is the federal standard.' Create a quick-reference card with state-specific timelines.

A: Self-awareness
B: Self-management
C: Responsible decision-making
D: Social awareness
CORRECT_Explanation: There are five well-known components of the CASEL model. Although all five components are important to teach students, the two CASEL components that are most important and relevant for this question's scenario are social awareness and relationship skills. C: Teach students what a bystander is and how they can impact peer interactions
D: Teach students how to be assertive
CORRECT_Explanation: All other response choices for this question are related to building specific skills that primarily impact individual student skills, but answer C is a skill that can impact the school's culture and environment. Direct and Indirect Services for Children, Families, and

This school district was sued because it violated the law regarding tracking students and denying access to courses for some students. In the final legal decision, the judge based his opinion on which landmark case?
A: Diane v. State Board of Education
B: Brown v. Board of Education
C: Hobson v. Hansen
D: Larry v. Riles

question_stem: A district-level school psychologist is asked to lead a threat assessment on a high school student. The student was caught at school with pepper spray and a list of students targeted for revenge. The threat assessment team was thorough during the inquiry and confidential student information was shared with the administration and law enforcement. Parents were not present during the interview with the student of concern. Because a violent incident was averted and strict disciplinary action was taken against the student of concern, the parents of the targeted students were not notified. What potential legal violation is involved in this situation?
A: Since law enforcement was given confidential student information, the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) may have been violated
B: A duty-to-warn infraction occurred
C: Informed consent by the parents was not secured before the threat assessment
D: A psychologist cannot conduct a threat assessment on a student without the student's parents or legal representation present
CORRECT_Explanation: Any time safety or danger is an issue, confidentiality laws can be suspended. Parents do not need to give consent or be present in a student interview if other students are in danger. However, if a list was discovered with student names on it and a weapon was found, the parents of the students who were listed must be notified. Precedent for this situation is based on the Tarasoff case. Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the learner must use scenario details in 'A district-level school psychologist is asked to lead a threat assessment on a high' to choose the best-fit intervention response. Success requires discriminating A duty-to-warn infraction occurred from plausible but mismatched alternatives like Since law enforcement was given confidential student information, the Family.
construct_actually_tested: Context-based discrimination of duty-to-warn infraction occurred within the clinical decision context of district-level school psychologist is asked to lead threat
rationale: Any time safety or danger is an issue, confidentiality laws can be suspended. Parents do not need to give consent or be present in a student interview if other students are in danger. However, if a list was discovered with student names on it and a weapon was found, the parents of the students who were listed must be notified. Precedent for this situation is based on the Tarasoff case. Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that sharing confidential information with law enforcement violates FERPA in all threat assessment situations.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: FERPA confidentiality exceptions for safety and law enforcement in threat situations
distractor_misconception_C: The student thought that informed parent consent is a prerequisite for conducting threat assessment interviews with students.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Threat assessment procedures overriding standard consent when student safety is involved
distractor_misconception_D: The student assumed that parental presence is legally required during threat assessment interviews.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Threat assessment protocols permitting parent exclusion when other students face danger
dominant_error_pattern: Practitioners share confidential student information with law enforcement during threat assessment while failing to notify the parents of *targeted* students of the threat, confusing FERPA exceptions for safety with the independent duty to warn potential victims.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A misidentifies FERPA as the legal violation (FERPA permits information sharing with law enforcement for safety). Choice C incorrectly asserts informed consent is required for threat assessment (it is not in emergencies). Choice D incorrectly requires parental presence during threat assessment interviews. Misconception: 'If we told law enforcement, we satisfied our obligations' or 'Since no violence occurred, we don't need to notify parents of targeted students.' Teach: *Duty-to-warn* is the legal obligation to notify potential victims/families of credible threats. *FERPA exception* permits sharing the student's educational record with law enforcement. These are distinct. Failing to notify the *targeted* students' parents (even though the threat was averted) is a *duty-to-warn violation*, regardless of FERPA compliance or law enforcement involvement.

question_stem: The National Association of School Psychologists (NASP) encourages school psychologists to engage in direct and indirect services for children, families, and schools. Which one of the following is considered a central guiding document for supporting all students?
A: The NASP code of ethical practice
B: Individuals With Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
C: The National Certified School Psychologist guidelines
D: Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA)

The principles of ESSA are found in the new NASP Practice Model. Answer A is a fictitious, but valid-sounding option. question_stem: School psychologists are often considered change agents in schools. They have knowledge and training in promoting system change. The evolution of change in the field of school psychology is observed in the movement from a focus on individual student deficits and remediation to which of the following?
A: A systems approach that supports all student learning, both academic and socialemotional, and provides indirect services
B: A direct approach to student intervention and accommodation
C: A consultation model that builds teacher skills
D: A school-level approach that focuses on prevention
CORRECT_Explanation: A systems approach that supports all student learning, both academic and social emotional, and provides indirect services The field of school psychology has been moving toward a "systems" approach to help all students, instead of the more narrow and traditional focus on special education students. question_stem: On most mainstream cognitive tests, a T-score of 64 is considered to be within which range?
A: Above average
B: Average
C: Below average
D: Significantly below average
CORRECT_Explanation: The answer is factual and straightforward. T-scores typically have an average range between 40 to 60. Make sure you do not confuse T-scores with standard scores (SS). If this question asked about an SS of 64, then the answer would be substantially different (e.g., SS = 64 is significantly below average). Practices That Permeate All Aspects of Service
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it requires retrieving the classification of T-score 64 as one standard deviation above the mean, placing it in above-average range.
construct_actually_tested: T-score range classification and above-average range identification on continuous score distribution scales
rationale: The answer is factual and straightforward. T-scores typically have an average range between 40 to 60. Make sure you do not confuse T-scores with standard scores (SS). distractor_misconception_B: The student believed that a T-score of 64 falls within the average range.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: Understanding T-score distribution ranges
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that a T-score of 64 is below the mean of 50.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Understanding T-score distribution
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that a T-score of 64 indicates a severe deficit.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Understanding T-score interpretation ranges
dominant_error_pattern: Examinees conflate T-score units with percentile ranks or standard deviations, miscalculating the distance of T=64 from the mean of 50 and misidentifying the resulting normative classification.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (average) treats T=64 as average despite SD=10 making 64 clearly 1.4 SD above the mean. Distractor C (below-average) reverses the direction, assuming 64 is below 50. Distractor D over-pathologizes above-average performance as significantly below average. Teach: T-score = 50 + 10z, so z = (64−50)/10 = 1.4 SD above mean. Show: 1.4 SD is firmly above average (average = ±1 SD). Use a labeled T-score ruler overlaid on a normal curve to visually anchor: T=50 at center, T=60 at +1 SD, T=70 at +2 SD; T=64 falls between center and +1 SD in the above-average region.

question_stem: Michele, a third-grade student, struggles to read at grade level. Teachers believe that Michele's reading skills are a full year below her grade. Michele's family is collaborative and they want direct support for Michele, but they are struggling financially and had to move twice in the past 2 years. The parents and teachers are worried that Michele will fall further behind in her reading development. In a consultation situation, what should the school psychologist recommend?
A: The psychologist should recommend a special education evaluation based on the Child Find law
B: The psychologist should recommend an after-school tutoring program and connect the family with community support services
C: The psychologist should make a formal recommendation to the school's response-to-intervention (RTI) team
D: The psychologist should tell the student's team to focus initially on building the student's phonologic processes
CORRECT_Explanation: Because the student has reading problems, a formal intervention should be recommended. Special education might be an option, but typically a referral to special education is made at a later time. Because Michele has moved twice at a young age, it is likely that there is an environmental factor influencing her learning. Answer D is valid, but we don't know if the student has phonologic difficulties without more information. Answer C is specific and likely to lead to formal supports for the student. Direct and Indirect
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must evaluate a realistic case of a struggling reader with family collaboration and barriers, then select the appropriate RTI-aligned response rather than premature special education referral.
construct_actually_tested: RTI-aligned response to reading difficulty with family collaboration and socioeconomic factors: intervention before special education evaluation
rationale: Because the student has reading problems, a formal intervention should be recommended. distractor_skill_deficit_A: RTI protocol prerequisite steps

distractor_skill_deficit_B: MTSS team approach methodology
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed phonological process focus is appropriate without prior problem analysis.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Assessment before intervention selection
dominant_error_pattern: Students recommend individual tutoring, special education referral, or isolated skill training without situating the case within an MTSS team problem-solving framework, missing that the appropriate recommendation is to bring the case to the RTI team for collaborative, data-based decision-making.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A shows students jumping to referral without RTI process, indicating they skip team-based problem-solving. Choice B shows students recommending individual tutoring as standalone intervention without team consultation, suggesting they don't recognize RTI team as gatekeeper. Choice D shows students prescribing a specific skill focus without prior problem-solving analysis, treating assumptions as data. Teach that the school psychologist's consultation role in RTI is to recommend team involvement: (1) Frame the case (Michele is 1 year below grade level in reading, family engaged, socioeconomic barriers present); (2) Ask: 'What has Tier 1 instruction looked like? Was it delivered with fidelity?' (3) Recommend to RTI team: collaborative problem-solving, possible Tier 2 small-group intervention, ongoing monitoring; (4) Connect family to community support services (within consultation, not instead of RTI). Have students role-play a teacher bringing Michele's case to a consultant, then have the consultant demonstrate recommending RTI team problem-solving rather than tutoring or referral, and identify which socioeconomic barriers fall outside RTI scope (for community linkage) versus which fall within it (instructional response).

Build a tier descriptor matrix: Tier 2 = 'Strategic Interventions for At-Risk Students' (approximately 5–8% of population, 3–4x/week, small group 4–6 students, in addition to Tier 1, delivered in general education). Contrast with Tier 1 (universal, all students, daily, within core instruction) and Tier 3 (intensive, very small group or individual, daily or multiple daily, high intensity). Have students read three intervention descriptions and label the tier, explicitly flagging when prevalence percentage is mentioned versus when intensity and instructional location matter more.

question_stem: A parent is demanding that her son be placed in an Individualized Education Program (IEP) because he has a formal diagnosis of schizophrenia. The student's pediatrician also recommended an IEP and wrote a prescription for one. Despite the mother's concern, the student's medication appears to be effective in managing his disorder and he is able to make educational progress at school. What is the school psychologist's response to the parent?
A: If there is no educational or social impact, then a formal IEP may not be appropriate
B: Legally, a medical diagnosis and recommendation from the doctor must be honored
C: Although the school is not legally obligated to provide an IEP in this situation, it is good practice to provide special education support
D: Students with schizophrenia should be provided formal supports and an IEP, especially to prevent the student's difficulties from getting worse within the school environment
CORRECT_Explanation: Special education qualifications have strict criteria that a special education team must consider. A primary consideration by the special education team is to determine whether a student cannot access the general curriculum due to a disability and if there is a negative social impact from the suspected disability In this case, it is stated that the student is making "reasonable educational progress." There may be other ways to address the concerns of the parent and the needs of the student outside of the special education progress (e.g., Multi-Tiered System of Supports [MTS9). Note that medical doctors who are not employed by a school district have no authority to dictate educational services. Practices
complexity_rationale: This is an Application item because the student must apply eligibility standards to a diagnosed student who is functioning adequately in school and determine whether IEP entitlement is legally warranted. The item requires impact-based eligibility reasoning.
construct_actually_tested: Eligibility determination despite medical diagnosis: necessity of documented educational or social impact to justify IDEA services

There may be other ways to address the concerns of the parent and the needs of the student outside of the special education progress (e.g., Multi-Tiered System of Supports [MTS9). instructional_red_flags: Distractors B (medical diagnosis mandates IEP), C (good practice but not required), D (diagnosis mandates prevention-based IEP). IDEA eligibility requires: (1) diagnosis in a recognized category AND (2) documented adverse effect on education or social adjustment. A student with schizophrenia who is well-medicated and making progress may not qualify if there is no functional impact at school. A pediatrician cannot 'prescribe' an IEP; only an IEP team can determine eligibility. Teach: 'Diagnosis is necessary but not sufficient. You also need functional impairment in education. No impact = no IDEA eligibility, even with medical diagnosis. Refer to 504 if needed for accommodations only.'

question_stem: Before administering a new psychologic assessment, it is reasonable for a school psychologist to do which of the following?
A: Complete a formal training course on the new test
B: Give the new test to students, but report the scores as informal measures until proficiency is achieved
C: Practice with the new test and be supervised by a colleague familiar with the test until proficient
D: Watch a training video and practice with the new test until proficient
CORRECT_Explanation: Practice with the new test and be supervised by a colleague familiar with the test until proficient This question can be answered by eliminating all other choices by careful examination of the invalid parts within each option. The best choice is answer C because this choice includes the critical "practice and supervision" components. A colleague can provide valuable insight and feedback when learning new psychologic tests or assessment tech- niques. It is not always feasible to take a full training course on a new test (answer A). Watching a video of a new assessment is helpful, but it does not provide an opportu- nity for interactive questions or feedback (answer D). Answer B is invalid. construct_actually_tested: Supervision standards, credential maintenance, scope-of-competence growth, and reflective practice; applied to 'Before administering a new psychologic assessment, it is reasonable for a school' scenario discrimination

question_stem: A school psychologist is reviewing research about several new assessments for reading fluency. As the psychologist reviews the reliability data, they note there is significant variability in the correlations. Which of the following correlation coefficients depicts the strongest reliability?
CORRECT_Explanation: Remember that a negative correlation can be stronger than a positive correlation. Note that a -0.97 is a very high and almost perfect (i.e., 1.0) correlation. Practices That Permeate
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks students to recognize which correlation coefficient represents the strongest reliability by comparing their magnitudes. The task requires applying the basic rule that absolute value indicates strength without scenario complexity.
construct_actually_tested: Comparison of reliability coefficient magnitudes by absolute value to identify strongest measurement stability evidence
rationale: Remember that a negative correlation can be stronger than a positive correlation. Note that a -0.97 is a very high and almost perfect (i.e., 1.0) correlation. Practices That Permeate
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that a high positive correlation indicates stronger reliability than a high negative correlation.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Understanding of correlation direction in reliability
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that any positive correlation indicates strong reliability.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Understanding correlation strength and direction
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that a negative correlation cannot indicate strong reliability.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Understanding correlation strength regardless of sign
dominant_error_pattern: Students select the positive coefficient 0.96 over the negative coefficient -0.97 based on sign alone, failing to recognize that reliability is defined by magnitude (absolute value) regardless of correlation direction.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A mistakes sign for strength—students believe positive correlations automatically outweigh negative ones. Choice C selects a near-zero coefficient—students may interpret low positive numbers as 'safe' or 'true reliability.' Choice D dismisses the negative coefficient as inherently weak—students overgeneralize directional bias without examining magnitude. Reliability depends on magnitude: 'A correlation of -0.97 is stronger (more reliable) than 0.96 because |−0.97| = 0.97 > 0.96. Sign indicates direction (negative relationship); magnitude indicates strength. In reading fluency, a -0.97 means as one measure increases, the other decreases strongly and consistently—that IS reliability if the constructs are inversely related. Don't skip the absolute value.'
primarySnippet: A correlation of -0.97 indicates a very strong negative relationship between variables

question_stem: A student has been identified with learning problems and is placed in a small group to help remediate the student's math difficulties. In which tier of the response-to-intervention (RTI) process would a school psychologist find this type of intervention?
A: Tier 1
B: Tier A
C: The formal intervention tier
D: Tier 2
CORRECT_Explanation: Answer D is the correct answer and it is synonymous with strategic intervention. At this level, data from Tier 1 is used to focus new interventions, typically in a small group.
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for accurate identification of which RTI tier includes small-group remedial instruction—Tier 2—a straightforward, factual tier-placement definition without requiring scenario analysis.
construct_actually_tested: Tier placement of small-group remedial intervention: distinguishing Tier 2 targeted support from Tier 1 universal and Tier 3 intensive
rationale: Answer D is the correct answer and it is synonymous with strategic intervention. At this level, data from Tier 1 is used to focus new interventions, typically in a small group.
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that small group remediation instruction occurs at Tier 1.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Small group placement Tier 2
distractor_misconception_B: The student conflated tier naming conventions with alphanumeric labeling systems for tiers.
distractor_skill_deficit_B: RTI numeric tier nomenclature
distractor_misconception_C: The student mistook informal tier naming conventions for the standard numeric tier numbering system in RTI.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Standard RTI tier numbering
dominant_error_pattern: Students place small-group remedial instruction at Tier 1 or use non-standard tier naming (A, 'formal intervention'), failing to recognize the foundational distinction that small-group targeted support—by definition—moves from universal to targeted and thus is Tier 2.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A shows students naming Tier 1 for a small-group intervention, indicating they don't understand that small-group instruction moves beyond universality and into targeting. Choice B reveals students using alphabetic tier naming instead of numeric, suggesting confusion with alternative classification systems or lack of RTI standard terminology. Choice C shows students using informal naming ('formal intervention tier') instead of standard numbering, indicating weak grasp of RTI nomenclature. Establish Tier 1 vs. Tier 2 boundary using group size and universality: Tier 1 = whole-class or classroom-wide instruction (all students receive it, no pulling out); Tier 2 = small group pulled from classroom (4–8 students not meeting benchmarks receive supplemental instruction). The moment a student is identified as below benchmark and placed in a small group for remediation (reading group, math intervention group), that IS Tier 2 by definition. Have students apply a quick rule: 'If the intervention is delivered to a small group identified as needing extra help PLUS they're still in general education, that's Tier 2.' Use case examples: 'Whole-class guided reading rotation' (Tier 1, all students participate in some rotation) versus 'Pull-out guided reading group for below-benchmark readers' (Tier 2, targeted).

question_stem: Two mainstream cogitive test batteries are the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC) and the Stanford-Binet. When these tests were being constructed, their overall full-scale scores were statistically analyzed and the scores were found to be highly correlated. What is the purpose of correlating overall scores on similar tests when creating them?
A: To establish validity
B: To establish convergent reliability
C: To establish predictive reliability
D: To establish the degree of similarity between tests
CORRECT_Explanation: When two similar measures are purposely correlated, this is done to establish validity. Two similar, but different tests should be highly correlated. If two similar tests do not correlate well, then one of the tests might be an invalid measure of the construct being assessed. Correlation coefficients for test validity are typically within the 0.70 to 0.90 range. Note that tests need both high validity and reliability, but both of these concepts
complexity_rationale: This is a Recall item because it asks for the purpose of correlating overall scores on similar tests, which is a factual concept requiring knowledge retrieval about test development methodology.
construct_actually_tested: Purpose of convergent validity evidence through inter-test correlations in standardized cognitive battery development
rationale: When two similar measures are purposely correlated, this is done to establish validity. Two similar, but different tests should be highly correlated. If two similar tests do not correlate well, then one of the tests might be an invalid measure of the construct being assessed. Correlation coefficients for test validity are typically within the 0.70 to 0.90 range. Note that tests need both high validity and reliability, but both of these concepts
distractor_misconception_A: The student believed that correlating scores directly establishes the validity of the tests.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: understanding of test validity versus reliability
distractor_misconception_C: The student believed that correlating scores predicts future performance.
distractor_skill_deficit_C: Predictive validity versus reliability distinction
distractor_misconception_D: The student believed that correlating scores is primarily about measuring similarity between tests.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: understanding of convergent reliability purpose
dominant_error_pattern: Students misidentify the purpose of correlating full-scale scores between major test batteries as a validity-establishment procedure or test similarity index, when it actually demonstrates convergent reliability through the inter-test correlation logic.
instructional_red_flags: Choice A conflates the inter-test correlation with validity establishment—students believe demonstrating correlation IS establishing validity, missing the specific reliability dimension. Choice C wrongly channels the correlation purpose toward predictive reliability (an uncommon term)—students invent terminology or confuse prediction with reliability. Choice D states the purpose as measuring test similarity—students interpret the correlation as a comparison tool rather than as evidence of consistent measurement. Clarify the term 'convergent reliability': 'When WISC and Stanford-Binet full-scale scores correlate highly, you have evidence that two different tests measuring similar ability constructs produce similar scores. That IS convergent reliability (or convergent validity, depending on the framework)—the instruments converge on the same outcome. It's not about whether one test is more reliable than the other; it's that they both measure the construct reliably enough that they correlate. The purpose is to validate that independent measures of the same construct agree.'
primarySnippet: Convergent validity is established when two mainstream cognitive tests correlate with each other

question_stem: School psychologists are increasingly asked to support students with several different types of difficulties, especially concussions. If a student sustains a hit to the back of the head and sustains a mild concussion, what difficulties should you expect and how should you support the student within the school environment?
A: The student will most likely have headaches, visual difficulties, and light sensitivity. The school psychologist should allow for frequent rest breaks and support the student at school as symptoms slowly resolve
B: The student will most likely have headaches. The school psychologist should allow the student to stay at home until the student's headaches have fully resolved
C: The student will most likely have fine motor difficulties and be prone to anger outbursts. The school psychologist should allow the student to stay home until all symptoms are resolved
D: The student will most likely have difficulty with talking and walking. The school psychologist should collaborate with parents and not allow the student to return until completely symptom free
CORRECT_Explanation: The student will most likely have headaches, visual difficulties, and light sensitiv- ity. The school psychologist should allow for frequent rest breaks and support the student at school as symptoms slowly resolve School psychologists do not have extensive training in neuropsychology, but they should be aware of basic brain-based functions. A basic knowledge of neuropsychology can increase a school psychologist's understanding of student learning and behavior. In this question, a hit to the back of the head typically impacts the occipital lobe of the brain. The occipital lobe is the primary visual cortex. When the occipital lobe is dysfunctional due to a concussion, students commonly have problems with visual processes (reading, writing) and they may experience light sensitivity. In addition to cognitive issues, a con- cussion typically produces frequent headaches. It is important to note that students with concussions should not stay home when symptoms are only mild; therefore, answers B, C, and D are not good choices. rationale: student at school as symptoms slowly resolve School psychologists do not have extensive training in neuropsychology, but they should be aware of basic brain-based functions. A basic knowledge of neuropsychology can increase a school psychologist's understanding of student learning and behavior. In this question, a hit to the back of the head typically impacts the occipital lobe of the brain. The occipital lobe is the primary visual cortex. When the occipital lobe is dysfunctional due to a concussion, students commonly have problems with visual processes (reading, writing) and they may experience light sensitivity. In addition to cognitive issues, a concussion typically produces frequent headaches. It is important to note that students with concussions should not stay home when symptoms are only mild; therefore, answers B, C, and D are not good choices. Emphasize school support (rest breaks, lighting adjustment) over home isolation for mild concussions.

question_stem: According to the Virginia Model of Threat Assessment, what are the three qualitative identification levels for threats?
A: Low, medium, and high
B: Nonsignificant, insignificant, and imminent
C: Transient, substantive, and imminent
D: Low, medium, and significant
CORRECT_Explanation: The Virginia Model of Threat Assessment (see Dr. Cornell's work) uses three qualitative categories for threats. The most current classifications for threat assessments are transient, substantive, and imminent. The older classifications, offered by the U.S. Secret Service, are low, medium, and high. distractor_misconception_A: The student confused the Virginia Model threat classification terminology, substituting severity levels for the actual classification categories.
distractor_skill_deficit_A: Virginia Model threat assessment three-level classification system terminology
distractor_misconception_B: The student conflated alternative threat classification schemes with the Virginia Model's specific categorical framework.

distractor_misconception_D: The student mistook quantitative severity scaling for the qualitative threat classification system in the Virginia Model.
distractor_skill_deficit_D: Virginia Model qualitative threat classification vs. numerical or ordinal severity scales
dominant_error_pattern: Learners substitute ordinal severity scales (low/medium/high) or alternative categorical frameworks for the Virginia Model's specific three-level qualitative threat classification (transient/substantive/imminent).
instructional_red_flags: Choice A conflates Virginia Model with generic numerical severity scales. Choice B invents unrelated categorical terminology. Choice D treats qualitative classification as subjective/interchangeable. Misconception: 'Threat levels are just low, medium, high, or the school can make up its own.' Teach Virginia Model terminology precisely: *Transient threat* = student expressed anger/frustration in a specific incident, no actual plan or intent, but may require brief follow-up/counseling. *Substantive threat* = student has intent + some capacity but may allow time for school intervention (e.g., vague access to means, early planning). *Imminent threat* = student poses immediate danger (weapon accessible, specific timing/location identified, no hesitation indicators). Each category triggers different response urgency. Use the exact Virginia Model language in documentation; do not substitute numerical scales or local terminology, as consistency ensures consistent interpretation across districts/contexts.
